entries.title: Breakfast China: Changsha Rice Noodles

segment.description: A husband and wife make rice noodles before sunrise in Changsha.
segment.description_zh: 一对夫妻天没亮就开始做米粉。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '早餐中国 长沙米粉'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a husband and wife making rice noodles before sunrise. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm rice noodles.
早餐 combines 早 (early) + 餐 (meal). You hear it in this segment, a husband and wife making rice noodles before.

entries.cultural_note: Changsha breakfast culture revolves around rice noodles. Shops open as early as 4 AM, and regulars have standing orders.
entries.follow_up: Each episode is only 5 minutes. Try watching 3-4 episodes back to back — the repetition of food vocabulary is natural spaced repetition.

entries.title: Breakfast China: Shandong Jianbing

segment.description: A street vendor makes jianbing crepes on a large griddle at a morning market.
segment.description_zh: 一个小摊在早市上做煎饼。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '早餐中国 煎饼'
search_terms: 早餐中国 山东煎饼

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a street vendor making jianbing crepes on a large griddle. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm egg.
鸡蛋 combines 鸡 (chicken) + 蛋 (egg). You hear it in this segment, a street vendor making jianbing crepes.

entries.cultural_note: Jianbing is one of China's most popular street breakfasts. The vendor's speed and rhythm are mesmerizing — each crepe takes under 90 seconds.
entries.follow_up: Watch the vendor's hands. Street food prep in China is performance art. The visual context carries you through vocabulary you don't yet know.

entries.title: Xi'an Muslim Quarter Street Food Walk

segment.description: A Douyin creator walks through the Muslim Quarter showing lamb skewers and pulled noodles.
segment.description_zh: 一个博主走在回民街上，拍羊肉串和拉面。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '西安回民街美食'
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search 'Xi'an Muslim Quarter street food'

questions.explanation: You can see a Douyin creator walking through the Muslim Quarter showing lamb — the Xi'an setting is clear from the opening shots.
多少钱 combines 多 (many) + 少 (few) + 钱 (money). You hear it in this segment, a Douyin creator walking through the Muslim Quarter.

entries.cultural_note: The Muslim Quarter in Xi'an has been a food district for over a thousand years. Halal lamb cuisine here predates most Western restaurant traditions.
entries.follow_up: Douyin clips are short and visually rich. Watch 5-6 Xi'an food clips in a row — you'll start recognizing repeated words like 好吃 and 来一个.

entries.title: Chengdu Night Market Walk

segment.description: A creator walks through a Chengdu night market, trying hotpot dipping sauces and malatang.
segment.description_zh: 一个博主逛成都夜市，吃火锅蘸料和麻辣烫。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '成都夜市'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a creator walking through a Chengdu night market. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm very spicy.
夜市 combines 夜 (night) + 市 (market/city). You hear it in this segment, a creator walking through a Chengdu night market.

entries.cultural_note: Chengdu night markets are social infrastructure. Families, couples, and friend groups eat side by side at communal tables — eating is never solitary here.
entries.follow_up: After watching, try ordering at a Chinese restaurant using just 我要一个 and pointing. The night market model of ordering is universal.

entries.title: Home With Kids: Meet the Family

segment.description: The blended family is introduced. The kids argue at the dinner table about household rules.
segment.description_zh: 一家人第一次一起吃饭，孩子们讨论家里的规矩。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '家有儿女 第一集'

questions.explanation: You can see the blended family being introduced — the at home setting is clear from the opening shots.
孩子 combines 孩 (child) + 子 (child/suffix). You hear it in this segment, the blended family being introduced.

entries.cultural_note: 家有儿女 is China's most beloved family sitcom, comparable to a warm American sitcom but with distinctly Chinese family dynamics — the parents' authority is real but constantly negotiated.
entries.follow_up: This show was designed for family audiences, so the dialogue is clear and repetitive. Great for building basic sentence patterns through sitcom binging.

entries.title: Home With Kids: Homework Battle

segment.description: The father tries to get the kids to do homework. Each child has a different excuse.
segment.description_zh: 爸爸让孩子们写作业，每个孩子都有不同的借口。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '家有儿女 第一集'
search_terms: 家有儿女 第一集 作业

questions.q_en: What does the dad want the kids to do?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the father trying to get the kids to do homework. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm do homework.
作业 combines 作 (work/do) + 业 (work/industry). You hear it in this segment, the father trying to get the kids.

questions.q_en: Do the kids want to do homework?
questions.explanation: Notice how the father trying to get the kids to do homework. Watch the characters' reactions — the answer becomes obvious.

entries.cultural_note: Homework pressure is a universal theme in Chinese family life. The comedy here resonates because every Chinese viewer has lived this exact scene.
entries.follow_up: Notice how the father uses 你们 (you all) vs 你 (you) — tracking plural/singular address is a free grammar lesson inside the comedy.

entries.title: The Bad Kids: Opening Scene
entries.title_zh: 隐秘的角落：第一集开场

segment.description: Three students discover something on a mountain hike. Tension builds through silence and landscape.
segment.description_zh: 三个学生在爬山时发现了不该看到的事。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '隐秘的角落 第一集'

questions.q_zh: 三个学生一开始在做什么？
questions.q_en: What are the three students doing at the start?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: three students discover something on a mountain hike. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm hiking.
发现 combines 发 (emit/send) + 现 (appear/present). You hear it in this segment, three students discover something on a mountain hike.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese suspense series open with slow observational tension, not action. The landscape does the emotional work before a single word of dialogue.
entries.follow_up: If you liked this, watch episodes 1-3. The pacing rewards patience. The children's Mandarin is clearer than most adult dialogue.

entries.title: The Bad Kids: Math Class

segment.description: The math teacher Zhang Dongsheng leads a class. His polite exterior hides something unsettling.
segment.description_zh: 数学老师张东升上课，表面很礼貌，但让人不安。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '隐秘的角落 第一集 上课'
search_terms: 隐秘的角落 张东升 上课

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the math teacher Zhang Dongsheng leading a class. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm math.
同学们 combines 同 (same) + 学 (study/learn) + 们 (plural marker). You hear it in this segment, the math teacher Zhang Dongsheng leading a class.
Pay attention to the delivery: the math teacher Zhang Dongsheng leading a class. The tone and body language reveal very polite.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese classroom scenes show institutional formality — students stand to greet the teacher, respond in unison. The ritual politeness here is weaponized by the plot.
entries.follow_up: Pay attention to how 同学们 is used as an address form. This is how every Chinese teacher opens a class. You'll hear it hundreds of times in real life.

entries.title: Day and Night: The Crime Scene

segment.description: Detective Guan arrives at a crime scene. He examines evidence and talks to colleagues about the case.
segment.description_zh: 关宏峰到达案发现场，检查证据，和同事讨论案情。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '白夜追凶 第一集'
search_terms: 白夜追凶 案发现场

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: detective Guan arriving at a crime scene. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm police detective.
证据 combines 证 (proof) + 据 (basis). You hear it in this segment, detective Guan arriving at a crime scene.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese crime procedurals show police work as deeply institutional — rank, protocol, and face management matter as much as deduction.
entries.follow_up: The twin-swap premise of this show is brilliant. If you like the first episode, the procedural vocabulary repeats enough to become comfortable by episode 3.

entries.title: Day and Night: The Interrogation

segment.description: Guan interrogates a suspect. The dialogue is tense, formal, and full of institutional language.
segment.description_zh: 关宏峰审问嫌疑人，对话紧张、正式，充满官话套话。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '白夜追凶 第一集 审问'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Guan interrogating a suspect. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm interrogating a suspect.
调查 combines 调 (investigate/tune) + 查 (check/examine). You hear it in this segment, Guan interrogating a suspect.
Notice how Guan interrogating a suspect. Watch the characters' reactions — the answer becomes obvious.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese interrogation scenes follow real procedural norms — recording equipment, formal address, and the suspect's right to silence are all shown. The formality is the tension.
entries.follow_up: Interrogation scenes are excellent for formal register practice. The vocabulary is specialized but the sentence patterns (你是不是..., 你为什么...) are universal.

entries.title: All Is Well: The Funeral

segment.description: The Su family gathers after the mother's death. Old resentments surface immediately among three siblings.
segment.description_zh: 苏家的母亲去世了，三个孩子聚在一起，旧怨立刻浮出水面。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '都挺好 第一集'

questions.explanation: The context makes this clear: the Su family gathering after the mother's death. That directly explains why the mother passed away is the answer.
难过 combines 难 (difficult) + 过 (pass/cross). You hear it in this segment, the Su family gathering after the mother's death.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese family dramas treat the family as a political system. Birth order, gender favoritism, and filial debt are structural forces, not just feelings.
entries.follow_up: This show became a national conversation about sexism in Chinese families. The emotional language is clear and the conflicts are universal enough to follow with intermediate Mandarin.

entries.title: All Is Well: The Family Dinner Argument

segment.description: The siblings argue over money and responsibility at dinner. The father refuses to take sides.
segment.description_zh: 兄妹在吃饭时为了钱和责任吵架，父亲不愿意表态。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '都挺好 第二集'
search_terms: 都挺好 第二集 吵架

questions.explanation: The context makes this clear: the siblings argue over money and responsibility at dinner. That directly explains why about money and responsibility is the answer.
公平 combines 公 (public) + 平 (level/fair). You hear it in this segment, the siblings argue over money.
Watch the segment closely: the siblings argue over money and responsibility at dinner. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm doesn't take a side.

entries.cultural_note: Arguments during family dinners are a signature of Chinese family drama. The dinner table is where power is negotiated — food is never just food in these scenes.
entries.follow_up: Track the word 应该 (should) in this scene. Each sibling has a different idea of what 'should' happen. It's a masterclass in obligation language.

entries.title: A Bite of China: Mountain Tofu
entries.title_zh: 舌尖上的中国：山里的豆腐

segment.description: A family in the mountains makes tofu by hand using traditional methods passed down for generations.
segment.description_zh: 山里的一家人用传统方法手工做豆腐，这个手艺传了好几代。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '舌尖上的中国 第一集'
search_terms: 舌尖上的中国 豆腐

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a family in the mountains making tofu by hand using. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm tofu.
传统 combines 传 (pass on) + 统 (system/unite). You hear it in this segment, a family in the mountains making tofu.

entries.cultural_note: A Bite of China redefined Chinese documentary. It treats food preparation as philosophy — the narrator's Mandarin is measured, literary, and perfectly enunciated.
entries.follow_up: The narration is HSK 4-5 level but spoken so clearly it's excellent listening practice. Turn on Chinese subtitles and read along.

entries.title: A Bite of China: Coastal Fishermen
entries.title_zh: 舌尖上的中国：海边渔民

segment.description: Fishermen in Fujian harvest seafood at dawn. Their wives prepare dishes that have defined the village for centuries.
segment.description_zh: 福建的渔民天亮前出海捕鱼，妻子们做几百年来的传统海鲜菜。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '舌尖上的中国 第二集'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '舌尖上的中国 海鲜'
search_terms: 舌尖上的中国 渔民

questions.q_en: When do the fishermen go to sea?
questions.explanation: In this 'A Bite of China' segment, fishermen in Fujian harvest seafood at dawn. The answer follows from what you see and hear.
新鲜 combines 新 (new) + 鲜 (fresh). You hear it in this segment, fishermen in Fujian harvest seafood at dawn.
You can see fishermen in Fujian harvest seafood at dawn — the fujian setting is clear from the opening shots.

entries.cultural_note: Coastal Chinese food culture is radically different from inland. The emphasis on freshness (新鲜) is almost obsessive — fish must be alive minutes before cooking.
entries.follow_up: Compare this episode's vocabulary with the mountain tofu segment. The contrast between coastal and inland food words maps China's geography through your vocabulary.

entries.title: Shanghai French Concession Walk

segment.description: A quiet walk through the French Concession area with ambient street sounds, occasional Mandarin conversations from passersby, and shots of cafes and plane trees.
segment.description_zh: 安静地走过法租界，听路人的中文对话，看咖啡店和梧桐树。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '上海法租界 city walk 4K'

questions.explanation: You can see a quiet walk through the French Concession area with ambient — the Shanghai setting is clear from the opening shots.
散步 combines 散 (scatter) + 步 (step). You hear it in this segment, a quiet walk through the French Concession area.

entries.cultural_note: City walk videos are a major genre on Chinese social media. The French Concession is Shanghai's most walkable neighborhood — its tree-lined streets feel more Parisian than Chinese, which is itself a cultural statement.
entries.follow_up: Use city walk videos as ambient listening. Don't try to understand everything — just let the street Mandarin wash over you. Your ear is training even when your brain isn't translating.

entries.title: Round Table: Why Chinese People Live to Eat
entries.title_zh: 圆桌派：中国人为什么为吃而活

segment.description: Dou Wentao and guests discuss why food occupies the center of Chinese social life. The conversation is witty and casual.
segment.description_zh: 窦文涛和嘉宾聊为什么中国人什么事都离不开饭局。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '圆桌派 吃'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Dou Wentao and guests discuss why food occupies the center. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm eating culture.
关系 combines 关 (close/related) + 系 (connect/system). You hear it in this segment, Dou Wentao and guests discuss why food occupies.

questions.q_zh: 在中国，吃饭最重要的意义是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the most important meaning of eating in China?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Dou Wentao and guests discuss why food occupies the center. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm social interaction.

entries.cultural_note: 圆桌派 is intellectual but never academic. Dou Wentao's hosting style is Socratic — he asks questions that make smart people say interesting things. The Mandarin is natural educated speech.
entries.follow_up: This show is ideal for bridging intermediate to advanced. The guests speak naturally but the host keeps things accessible. Start with food-related episodes since you already have the vocabulary base.

entries.title: Round Table: What Makes a City Livable
entries.title_zh: 圆桌派：什么样的城市适合生活

segment.description: The panel debates what makes cities livable — comparing Beijing's scale, Shanghai's style, and Chengdu's pace.
segment.description_zh: 嘉宾讨论什么样的城市适合生活，比较北京的规模、上海的风格和成都的节奏。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '圆桌派 城市'

questions.explanation: Pay attention to the delivery: the panel debates what making cities livable. The tone and body language reveal beijing, shanghai, chengdu.
节奏 means pace/rhythm. Listen for it in this 'Round Table' segment, the panel debates what making cities livable —.

entries.cultural_note: The Beijing vs Shanghai vs Chengdu debate is a national pastime. Each city represents a different life philosophy — ambition, sophistication, and ease, respectively.
entries.follow_up: After watching, try describing your own city using the comparison structures you heard: 比 (compared to), 没有...那么 (not as...as), 更适合 (more suitable for).

entries.title: The Thirteen Invitations: Jia Zhangke on Small Towns
entries.title_zh: 十三邀：贾樟柯谈小城市

segment.description: Xu Zhiyuan interviews filmmaker Jia Zhangke about growing up in Fenyang and why small-town China matters to his art.
segment.description_zh: 许知远采访导演贾樟柯，聊他在汾阳长大的经历以及小城市对他创作的影响。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '十三邀 贾樟柯'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: xu Zhiyuan interviewing filmmaker Jia Zhangke about growing up. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm film director.
记忆 combines 记 (record) + 忆 (recall). You hear it in this segment, xu Zhiyuan interviewing filmmaker Jia Zhangke about growing.

questions.q_zh: 他们讨论什么对贾樟柯的创作有影响？
questions.q_en: What do they discuss as influencing Jia Zhangke's art?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: xu Zhiyuan interviewing filmmaker Jia Zhangke about growing up. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm small town life.

entries.cultural_note: Jia Zhangke is China's most important living filmmaker for depicting ordinary life. His Mandarin carries a Shanxi accent — regional speech is real Chinese, not a deficiency.
entries.follow_up: If this interview interests you, watch Jia Zhangke's film 山河故人 (Mountains May Depart). His visual style tells you what to feel before the dialogue does.

entries.title: The Thirteen Invitations: On Being an Intellectual Today

segment.description: Xu Zhiyuan asks a scholar about the role of intellectuals in contemporary China. The conversation is measured and revealing.
segment.description_zh: 许知远和一位学者讨论知识分子在当代中国的角色，对话克制但很有深度。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '十三邀 知识分子'

questions.q_zh: 他们讨论的核心话题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the core topic they discuss?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: xu Zhiyuan asking a scholar about the role of intellectuals. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the role of intellectuals.
当代 means contemporary. Listen for it in this 'The Thirteen Invitations' segment, xu Zhiyuan asking a scholar about the role.

entries.cultural_note: 许知远's interview style is deliberately awkward. He asks questions that make guests uncomfortable in productive ways. The silences are as important as the words.
entries.follow_up: 十三邀 is where Chinese public intellectualism lives now. Each episode is a full hour — use this 8-minute clip as an entry point and return when your listening stamina grows.

entries.title: A Lifelong Journey: The Neighborhood in Winter

segment.description: 1969, northeast China. The Zhou family prepares for two children to leave for the countryside. Snow, small rooms, and quiet dread.
segment.description_zh: 1969年，东北。周家准备送两个孩子下乡。雪、小房间、安静的不安。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '人世间 第一集'

questions.q_en: When does the story take place?
questions.explanation: You can see 1969, northeast China. The Zhou family prepares for two children — the 1969 setting is clear from the opening shots.
离开 means leave. Listen for it in this 'A Lifelong Journey' segment, 1969, northeast China.
You can see 1969, northeast China. The Zhou family prepares for two children — the the countryside setting is clear from the opening shots.

entries.cultural_note: The sent-down youth (下乡) movement shaped an entire generation. 人世间 is the rare show that treats this history with nuance rather than propaganda or melodrama.
entries.follow_up: This show spans 50 years of Chinese history through one family. The first episode's vocabulary is domestic and emotional — excellent for learning how Chinese families actually talk to each other.

entries.title: Bilibili: Shanghai's Hidden Architecture

segment.description: A Bilibili creator explains the history behind Shanghai's shikumen lane houses and Art Deco buildings with on-location footage.
segment.description_zh: 一个B站UP主实地讲解上海石库门和装饰艺术建筑的历史。

where_to_find.primary: Bilibili: search '上海建筑历史'

questions.q_zh: 视频讲的是什么城市的建筑？
questions.q_en: What city's architecture does the video cover?
questions.explanation: You can see a Bilibili creator explaining the history behind Shanghai's shikumen lane — the Shanghai setting is clear from the opening shots.
建筑 combines 建 (build) + 筑 (construct). You hear it in this segment, a Bilibili creator explaining the history behind Shanghai's.

entries.cultural_note: Bilibili explainer videos (科普) are China's equivalent of well-produced YouTube essays. The creators are often obsessive enthusiasts, not professionals — which makes the language more natural.
entries.follow_up: Bilibili's danmu (bullet comments) flying across the screen are distracting at first but eventually become a reading exercise. Try keeping them on for 2 minutes, then off.

entries.title: Ming Dynasty 1566: The Court Assembly

segment.description: The morning court assembly. Officials debate fiscal crisis while navigating the emperor's unpredictable moods. Every sentence carries double meaning.
segment.description_zh: 早朝上，大臣们讨论财政危机，同时小心应对皇帝喜怒无常。每句话都有弦外之音。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '大明王朝1566 第一集'

questions.q_zh: 大臣们在讨论什么问题？
questions.q_en: What problem are the ministers discussing?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the morning court assembly. Officials debate fiscal crisis while navigating. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm fiscal crisis.
朝廷 means imperial court. Listen for it in this 'Ming Dynasty 1566' segment, the morning court assembly.

questions.q_zh: 大臣们说话时最注意什么？
questions.q_en: What are the ministers most careful about when speaking?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the morning court assembly. Officials debate fiscal crisis while navigating. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the emperor's reaction.

entries.cultural_note: This show is considered the greatest Chinese political drama ever made. The dialogue is literary Mandarin — formal, layered, and precisely calibrated. Every line is a chess move.
entries.follow_up: Don't try to understand everything. Watch for body language, pauses, and reactions. The political dynamics are legible even when the classical vocabulary isn't. Return to this when you're at HSK 6+.

entries.title: Ming Dynasty 1566: Hai Rui Speaks Truth

segment.description: The incorruptible official Hai Rui confronts corruption directly while everyone else speaks in euphemisms. His bluntness is revolutionary.
segment.description_zh: 清官海瑞直接揭露腐败，而其他人都在绕弯子。他一句话说到底，不留余地。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '大明王朝1566 海瑞'

questions.q_zh: 海瑞和其他官员有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How is Hai Rui different from other officials?
questions.explanation: In this 'Ming Dynasty 1566' segment, the incorruptible official Hai Rui confronts corruption directly while everyone. The answer follows from what you see and hear.
腐败 means corruption. Listen for it in this 'Ming Dynasty 1566' segment, the incorruptible official Hai Rui confronts corruption directly.

entries.cultural_note: Hai Rui is one of the most famous figures in Chinese political culture — the honest official who speaks truth to power. His name is still invoked in modern political commentary.
entries.follow_up: The contrast between Hai Rui's plain speech and everyone else's elaborate indirection is a lesson in Chinese communication itself. Directness is marked; indirection is default.

entries.title: Yi Yi: NJ Walks Taipei at Night
entries.title_zh: 一一：NJ夜晚走在台北街头
entries.content_name_en: Yi Yi (A One and a Two)

segment.description: NJ walks through nighttime Taipei after reconnecting with a former lover. He speaks quietly about what his life has become.
segment.description_zh: NJ和初恋重逢后走在台北夜晚的街头，轻声说着这些年。

where_to_find.primary: Criterion Channel or Apple TV rental
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '一一 Edward Yang full movie' (sometimes available)

questions.q_zh: NJ在这个场景中的心情是什么？
questions.q_en: What is NJ's mood in this scene?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: nJ walking through nighttime Taipei after reconnecting with a former. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm quiet melancholy.
后悔 means regret. Listen for it in this 'Yi Yi (A One and a Two)' segment, nJ walking through nighttime Taipei after reconnecting.

questions.q_en: Who did NJ see before walking the streets?
questions.explanation: In this 'Yi Yi (A One and a Two)' segment, nJ walking through nighttime Taipei after reconnecting with a former. Listen for how a former.

entries.cultural_note: Edward Yang's 一一 is often called the greatest film about family ever made. The Mandarin is Taiwanese-inflected — slightly softer and more musical than mainland standard. It teaches you that Mandarin has texture, not just content.
entries.follow_up: This film is 3 hours long and rewards every minute. Watch it in 30-minute segments. The boy Yang-Yang's scenes are linguistically simpler and just as profound.

entries.title: Happy Together: Cooking in Buenos Aires
entries.title_zh: 春光乍泄：在布宜诺斯艾利斯做饭

segment.description: Lai Yiu-fai cooks for the injured Ho Po-wing in their Buenos Aires apartment. Quiet domestic routine between two people who can't quite let go.
segment.description_zh: 黎耀辉在布宜诺斯艾利斯的公寓里给受伤的何宝荣做饭。两个放不下彼此的人之间安静的日常。

where_to_find.primary: Criterion Channel or physical media
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '春光乍泄 Wong Kar-wai' (clips sometimes available)

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: lai Yiu-fai cooks for the injured Ho Po-wing. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm cooking.
照顾 means take care of. Listen for it in this 'Happy Together' segment, lai Yiu-fai cooks for the injured Ho Po-wing.

entries.cultural_note: Wong Kar-wai uses Cantonese and Mandarin interchangeably. This film's language is sparse — the characters communicate more through silence, food, and proximity than through dialogue.
entries.follow_up: The cooking scene is dialogue-light but emotionally dense. Focus on tone of voice rather than vocabulary. At advanced levels, how someone says something matters more than what they say.

entries.title: Behind the Headlines: Social Media and Reality
entries.title_zh: 锵锵三人行：社交媒体与现实
entries.content_name_en: Behind the Headlines with Wen Tao

segment.description: Dou Wentao and two guests debate how social media has changed public discourse in China. The conversation is fast, witty, and full of cultural references.
segment.description_zh: 窦文涛和两位嘉宾讨论社交媒体如何改变了中国的公共话语，对话快速、机智，充满文化典故。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '锵锵三人行 社交媒体'
search_terms: 锵锵三人行 社交媒体

questions.q_zh: 他们讨论社交媒体对什么的影响？
questions.q_en: What impact of social media do they discuss?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Dou Wentao and two guests debate how social media has. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm public discourse.
舆论 means public opinion. Listen for it in this 'Behind the Headlines with Wen Tao' segment, Dou Wentao and two guests debate how social.

questions.q_zh: 对话的风格是什么样的？
questions.q_en: What is the style of the conversation?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Dou Wentao and two guests debate how social media has. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm fast and witty.

entries.cultural_note: 锵锵三人行 ran for 19 years and was the most influential talk show in the Chinese-speaking world. Its cancellation in 2017 was itself a lesson in media and institutions.
entries.follow_up: This is peak-difficulty listening. Use it as a benchmark — revisit the same clip every 3 months and measure how much more you catch. Progress on hard material is the truest measure.

entries.title: CCTV Reader: On Lu Xun's Legacy

segment.description: A literary scholar discusses Lu Xun's continuing relevance. The language is formal, literary, and dense with classical allusions.
segment.description_zh: 一位文学学者讨论鲁迅的当代意义，语言正式、文学化，充满古典典故。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '读书 CCTV 鲁迅'

questions.explanation: In this 'CCTV Reader (Du Shu)' segment, a literary scholar discussing Lu Xun's continuing relevance. The answer follows from what you see and hear.
批判 means critique. Listen for it in this 'CCTV Reader (Du Shu)' segment, a literary scholar discussing Lu Xun's continuing relevance.

questions.q_zh: 这个节目的语言风格是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the language style of this program?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a literary scholar discussing Lu Xun's continuing relevance. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm formal and literary.

entries.cultural_note: Lu Xun is the most important modern Chinese writer. Knowing his name and basic ideas (critical of tradition, champion of vernacular Chinese) is cultural literacy — every educated Chinese person has opinions about him.
entries.follow_up: This is aspirational content. Don't expect to understand it now. Bookmark it and return in a year. When you can follow 60% of this, your Mandarin has crossed into genuine fluency.

entries.title: Street Vendor Making Jianbing

segment.description: A vendor spreads batter, cracks an egg, adds sauce and crunchy crackers.
segment.description_zh: 摊主摊面糊、打鸡蛋、加酱和脆饼。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '煎饼果子 制作'

questions.explanation: 鸡蛋 combines 鸡 (chicken) + 蛋 (egg). You hear it in this segment, a vendor spreads batter, cracks an egg.

entries.cultural_note: Jianbing is China's most popular breakfast street food — over a billion are eaten annually. Every vendor has their own style.
entries.follow_up: Next time you see a jianbing video, try to catch the vendor's patter. They often narrate what they're doing.

entries.title: Dumpling Folding Close-Up

segment.description: Hands fold dumplings at speed — fill, pinch, crimp, line up on tray.
segment.description_zh: 双手快速包饺子——放馅、捏边、摆盘。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '包饺子 手工'

questions.q_en: What is being made in the video?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: hands fold dumplings at speed. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm dumplings.

entries.cultural_note: Dumpling-making is communal — Chinese families gather around the table to fold them together, especially during Spring Festival.
entries.follow_up: Watch for the pinching technique. There are regional styles — northern dumplings are usually boiled (水饺), southern ones often pan-fried (锅贴).

entries.title: Chinese Breakfast Routine

segment.description: A montage of typical Chinese breakfast items: soy milk, fried dough, congee, steamed buns.
segment.description_zh: 典型中国早餐蒙太奇：豆浆、油条、粥、馒头。

where_to_find.primary: TikTok: search '#中国早餐 #chinesebreakfast'

questions.explanation: 豆浆 means soy milk. Listen for it in this 'Breakfast Routine' segment, a montage of typical Chinese breakfast items: soy.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese breakfast is savory, not sweet. The default morning drink is hot soy milk or congee, not coffee — though that's changing in big cities.
entries.follow_up: Try ordering 豆浆 and 油条 at a Chinese restaurant. It's the classic pairing.

entries.title: Morning Walk Through a Wet Market

segment.description: Walking through a busy morning market — vegetables, meat, tofu, vendors calling out prices.
segment.description_zh: 走过热闹的早市——蔬菜、肉、豆腐，摊主喊价钱。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '菜市场 早市'

questions.q_zh: 这个视频是在哪里拍的？
questions.explanation: You can see walking through a busy morning market — the a wet market setting is clear from the opening shots.
新鲜 combines 新 (new) + 鲜 (fresh). You hear it in this segment, walking through a busy morning market.

entries.cultural_note: Wet markets are still how most Chinese families shop for food daily. The habit of buying fresh each morning is deeply ingrained.
entries.follow_up: Listen for the numbers — vendors calling out prices is great ear training for Chinese numbers.

entries.title: Neon Signs of Chinese Cities at Night

segment.description: Slow panning shots of neon signs on Chinese storefronts — pharmacy, restaurant, hotel, massage.
segment.description_zh: 中国店铺霓虹灯慢镜头——药店、饭店、旅馆、按摩。

where_to_find.primary: Instagram Reels: search '#chinaneon #chinesecharacters'

questions.explanation: 药店 means pharmacy. Listen for it in this 'City Neon Signs' segment, slow panning shots of neon signs on Chinese.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese neon signs are hanzi reading practice hiding in plain sight. The most common ones (药店, 超市, 饭店) are high-frequency characters you'll see everywhere.
entries.follow_up: Pause the video and try to read each sign. You'll be surprised how many characters you already know.

entries.title: Tea Preparation in a Chengdu Teahouse

segment.description: A teahouse server pours tea from a long-spouted kettle, old men play mahjong in the background.
segment.description_zh: 茶馆服务员用长嘴壶倒茶，老人在旁边打麻将。

where_to_find.primary: TikTok: search '#成都茶馆 #chengdutea'
where_to_find.alt: Douyin: search '成都茶馆 盖碗茶'

questions.explanation: You can see a teahouse server pouring tea from a long-spouted kettle — the a teahouse setting is clear from the opening shots.

entries.cultural_note: Chengdu teahouses are the city's living rooms. People spend entire afternoons there — chatting, playing mahjong, doing nothing. It's the opposite of hustle culture.
entries.follow_up: The long-spouted kettle pour is called 长嘴壶 — it's both practical and performative. Watch for the server's technique.

entries.title: Noodle Pulling Technique

segment.description: A noodle master stretches dough into thin strands — fold, pull, slap against the counter.
segment.description_zh: 拉面师傅把面团拉成细条——折、拉、摔在案板上。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '拉面 手工'

questions.explanation: 面 means noodles. Listen for it in this 'Hand-Pulled Noodles' segment, a noodle master stretches dough into thin strands.

entries.cultural_note: 兰州拉面 (Lanzhou hand-pulled noodles) shops are everywhere in China — they're the country's most ubiquitous restaurant chain, all independently owned.
entries.follow_up: The rhythm of noodle pulling is meditative. Count the folds — each one doubles the number of strands.

entries.title: Rainy Day in Shanghai

segment.description: Rain on a Shanghai lane — umbrellas, reflections on wet pavement, a bicycle passing.
segment.description_zh: 上海弄堂的雨——雨伞、湿路面的倒影、一辆自行车经过。

where_to_find.primary: Instagram Reels: search '#shanghairain #上海下雨'
where_to_find.alt: TikTok: search '上海 下雨 街景'

questions.explanation: In this 'Shanghai Rain' segment, rain on a Shanghai lane. The answer follows from what you see and hear.

entries.cultural_note: Shanghai's 弄堂 (lane houses) are the city's architectural soul — narrow alleys lined with laundry, bikes, and neighbors chatting. They're rapidly disappearing to redevelopment.
entries.follow_up: Ambient city videos are underrated for language learning. The signage, background chatter, and atmosphere all build familiarity with the sound of Chinese life.

entries.title: Office Life Comedy Sketch

segment.description: A worker tries to leave on time but keeps getting pulled into 'one more thing' by the boss.
segment.description_zh: 一个员工想准时下班，但不断被老板叫住说'还有一件事'。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '办公室搞笑 下班'

questions.q_en: What does the employee want to do?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a worker trying to leave on time. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm leave on time.
加班 means work overtime. Listen for it in this 'Office Life Sketch' segment, a worker trying to leave on time.

entries.cultural_note: 996 culture (9am-9pm, 6 days a week) is a running joke — and real grievance — in Chinese internet culture. These sketches are social commentary disguised as comedy.
entries.follow_up: Chinese workplace humor is surprisingly universal. The comedy comes from the gap between what people say (当然可以 'of course') and what they mean (absolutely not).

entries.title: Beijing Hutong Bike Ride

segment.description: POV bike ride through narrow hutong alleys — old doorways, kids playing, a cat on a wall.
segment.description_zh: 第一视角在胡同骑车——老门洞、孩子们玩耍、墙上一只猫。

where_to_find.primary: TikTok: search '#beijinghutong #胡同骑行'
where_to_find.alt: Douyin: search '胡同 骑车 第一视角'

questions.explanation: In this 'Hutong Bike Ride' segment, pOV bike ride through narrow hutong alleys. The answer follows from what you see and hear.

entries.cultural_note: Beijing's hutongs are the old city's circulatory system — narrow alleys connecting courtyard houses (四合院). Many date back to the Yuan Dynasty (1200s).
entries.follow_up: Hutong POV videos are the best way to get a feel for Beijing's texture without being there. Watch for the red doors — they indicate the owner's historical status.

entries.title: Night Market Vendor Banter

segment.description: A customer haggles with a skewer vendor — playful back-and-forth about price and spice level.
segment.description_zh: 顾客和烤串摊主讨价还价——关于价格和辣度的趣味对话。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '夜市 砍价 搞笑'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a customer haggles with a skewer vendor. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the price.

entries.cultural_note: Haggling at night markets is half transaction, half performance. The vendor expects it — and the banter is part of the experience.
entries.follow_up: The phrase 便宜点 (piányi diǎn — 'make it cheaper') is one of the most useful things you can learn for traveling in China.

entries.title: Morning Tai Chi in the Park

segment.description: An elderly man practices tai chi at dawn, narrating each movement in simple Mandarin.
segment.description_zh: 一位老人在清晨练太极，用简单的中文解说每个动作。

where_to_find.primary: Instagram Reels: search '#taichi #太极拳 #morningroutine'
where_to_find.alt: Douyin: search '太极拳 公园 早晨'

questions.explanation: 早晨 means early morning. Listen for it in this 'Park Tai Chi' segment, an elderly man practices tai chi at dawn.

entries.cultural_note: Morning park exercise is a defining feature of Chinese urban life. Tai chi, dancing, badminton — parks come alive before 7am.
entries.follow_up: Chinese parks in the morning are a window into a different rhythm of life. If you ever visit China, go to a park at 6am.

entries.title: Tiny Chinese Apartment Tour

segment.description: A young worker shows their 20sqm apartment — fold-out desk, stacked storage, kitchen in a closet.
segment.description_zh: 一个年轻人展示20平米的公寓——折叠桌、叠放收纳、壁橱里的厨房。

where_to_find.primary: TikTok: search '#chineseapartment #小公寓'

questions.q_zh: 这个公寓最大的特点是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the most notable feature of this apartment?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a young worker showing their 20sqm apartment. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm very small.

entries.cultural_note: Urban housing in Chinese cities is tiny and expensive. Young workers in Beijing or Shanghai often live in 15-30sqm studios. The creativity of space optimization is remarkable.
entries.follow_up: These apartment tours teach you domestic vocabulary fast — 床 (bed), 桌子 (desk), 衣柜 (wardrobe) — all in context.

entries.title: Hospital Waiting Room Comedy

segment.description: A patient navigates the hospital registration system — wrong window, wrong floor, wrong department, full circle.
segment.description_zh: 病人在医院挂号——走错窗口、走错楼层、走错科室，转了一圈回到原点。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '医院挂号 搞笑'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a patient navigating the hospital registration system. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm can't find the right department.
排队 means queue/line up. Listen for it in this 'Hospital Comedy Sketch' segment, a patient navigating the hospital registration system —.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese hospitals are navigational mazes. You register at one window, pay at another, get tests at a third, then return results to a fourth. It's institutional comedy that writes itself.
entries.follow_up: The word 挂号 is essential if you ever need medical care in China. The system is efficient once you understand it, but the learning curve is steep.

entries.title: Subway Commuter Observations

segment.description: Rush hour observations — everyone on phones, someone asleep standing up, a kid doing homework.
segment.description_zh: 早高峰观察——人人看手机、有人站着睡着了、一个孩子在做作业。

where_to_find.primary: TikTok: search '#地铁日常 #subwaylife'
where_to_find.alt: Douyin: search '地铁 早高峰 搞笑'

questions.q_zh: 大部分人在地铁上做什么？
questions.q_en: What are most people doing on the subway?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: rush hour observations — everyone on phones. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm looking at phones.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese subway systems are world-class — clean, fast, cheap. Beijing and Shanghai's networks are among the largest on Earth. Rush hour is intense but orderly.
entries.follow_up: Subway announcements are great listening practice. They repeat the same patterns at every stop: 下一站 (next stop), 请注意安全 (please mind your safety).

entries.title: Vendor Wrapping Zongzi

segment.description: Expert hands wrap sticky rice in bamboo leaves — fold, fill, tie with string.
segment.description_zh: 巧手把糯米包在竹叶里——折叶、填米、绑绳。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '包粽子 手工'

questions.explanation: 粽子 means rice dumpling. Listen for it in this 'Zongzi Wrapping' segment, expert hands wrap sticky rice in bamboo leaves.

entries.cultural_note: Zongzi are eaten during the Dragon Boat Festival (端午节). There's a north-south divide: northerners like sweet (red bean), southerners like savory (pork).
entries.follow_up: The sweet vs savory zongzi debate is China's equivalent of the pineapple-on-pizza argument. Ask any Chinese person which they prefer.

entries.title: Government Office Bureaucracy Skit

segment.description: A citizen tries to get a document stamped — needs form A, which requires form B, which requires form A.
segment.description_zh: 市民想盖章——需要表格A，但表格A需要表格B，表格B又需要表格A。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '办事大厅 搞笑 盖章'

questions.q_en: What problem does the citizen encounter?

options.text: 陷入循环——每个表格需要另一个表格
options.text_en: Caught in a loop — each form requires another

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a citizen trying to get a document stamped. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm caught in a loop — each form.
盖章 means stamp/seal. Listen for it in this 'Government Office Skit' segment, a citizen trying to get a document stamped.

entries.cultural_note: The circular bureaucracy joke (踢皮球 — 'kick the ball around') is one of China's most beloved comedy genres. It resonates because everyone has lived it.
entries.follow_up: The word 盖章 (stamp) is surprisingly important in Chinese life. Official documents need red stamps (公章) to be valid — signatures alone aren't enough.

entries.title: Courtyard House Architecture Tour

segment.description: Close-up details of a traditional courtyard house — carved wooden screens, stone lions, roof tiles, symmetry.
segment.description_zh: 四合院建筑细节特写——雕花木屏、石狮子、瓦片、对称布局。

where_to_find.primary: TikTok: search '#siheyuan #四合院 #chinesearchitecture'
where_to_find.alt: Douyin: search '四合院 建筑 细节'

questions.q_zh: 四合院最重要的设计原则是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the key design principle of a siheyuan?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: close-up details of a traditional courtyard house. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm symmetry.

entries.cultural_note: The 四合院 (four-sided courtyard house) embodies Chinese spatial philosophy: inward-facing, hierarchical (the north room is for the patriarch), and centered on open sky.
entries.follow_up: Look for how the architecture encodes family structure — who lives in which room reflects their status. Architecture is sociology in stone.

entries.title: Street Interview: What Do You Think?

segment.description: Random people answer 'What makes you happy?' — answers range from funny to philosophical.
segment.description_zh: 随机路人回答'什么让你开心？'——答案从搞笑到哲学。

where_to_find.primary: Douyin: search '街头采访 什么让你开心'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: random people answer 'What making you happy?'. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm what makes you happy?.
生活 combines 生 (life/born) + 活 (live/alive). You hear it in this segment, random people answer 'What making you happy?' —.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese street interviews reveal the gap between official discourse and how people actually think. The best ones catch genuine, unscripted moments.
entries.follow_up: Street interviews are gold for listening practice — real people, real accents, real speech speed. Much harder than TV dialogue, but much more useful.

entries.title: Chinese Internet Slang in 60 Seconds

segment.description: A fast explainer of popular internet terms: 内卷, 躺平, 摸鱼, 社死 — with examples.
segment.description_zh: 快速讲解热门网络用语：内卷、躺平、摸鱼、社死——配例句。

where_to_find.primary: Bilibili: search '网络用语 科普 2024'
search_terms: 内卷 躺平 解释

options.text: 放弃竞争，选择轻松生活
options.text_en: Opt out of competition, choose easy life

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a fast explainer of popular internet terms: 内卷, 躺平. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm opt out of competition, choose easy.
摸鱼 means slack off (lit: catch fish). Listen for it in this 'Internet Slang Explainer' segment, a fast explainer of popular internet terms: 内卷.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese internet slang evolves at blinding speed. 内卷 and 躺平 became defining terms for an entire generation's attitude toward work culture.
entries.follow_up: These terms show up constantly in Chinese social media. Learning them is like learning the vocabulary of an entire worldview.

entries.title: Wuhan Night Market Walking Tour

segment.description: Walking through Wuhan's Hubu Lane night market — dozens of stalls, steam rising, crowds.
segment.description_zh: 走过武汉户部巷夜市——几十个摊位、蒸汽升腾、人潮涌动。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '武汉夜市 walking tour'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '武汉 户部巷 夜市'

questions.q_zh: 这个视频是什么时候拍的？
questions.explanation: In this 'Wuhan Night Market Tour' segment, walking through Wuhan's Hubu Lane night market. The answer follows from what you see and hear.
夜市 combines 夜 (night) + 市 (market/city). You hear it in this segment, walking through Wuhan's Hubu Lane night market —.

entries.cultural_note: Wuhan's food scene is one of China's best. The city's breakfast culture (过早) alone has hundreds of varieties — it's a city that takes eating seriously.
entries.follow_up: Night market walking tours are great passive listening — you hear vendor calls, customer orders, and general chatter at natural speed.

entries.title: Beijing Hutong Morning Routine

segment.description: Early morning in a hutong — a man walks his bird cage, neighbors greet each other, a vendor sets up.
segment.description_zh: 胡同的清晨——一个人提着鸟笼散步，邻居互相打招呼，摊贩开始摆摊。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '北京胡同 早晨 vlog'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '胡同 日常 早晨'

questions.q_en: What is the old man doing?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: early morning in a hutong. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm walking with a bird cage.
邻居 combines 邻 (neighbor) + 居 (reside). You hear it in this segment, early morning in a hutong.

entries.cultural_note: Bird-walking is a classic Beijing elder hobby. Men carry ornate cages to the park, hang them in trees, and let their birds sing to each other. It's social networking, 1950s style.
entries.follow_up: Hutong mornings have a rhythm you can feel even without understanding every word. The greetings — 吃了吗？(have you eaten?) — are social glue.

entries.title: Chinese Convenience Store Tour

segment.description: A tour of a Chinese convenience store — tea eggs, onigiri with Chinese fillings, hot oden, mobile payment.
segment.description_zh: 中国便利店探店——茶叶蛋、中式饭团、关东煮、手机支付。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search 'Chinese convenience store tour'

questions.q_zh: 在中国便利店怎么付钱？
questions.q_en: How do you pay at a Chinese convenience store?
questions.explanation: Pay attention to the delivery: a tour of a Chinese convenience store. The tone and body language reveal mobile payment.
便利店 means convenience store. Listen for it in this 'Convenience Store Tour' segment, a tour of a Chinese convenience store —.

entries.cultural_note: Mobile payment has completely replaced cash in urban China. Even street vendors use WeChat Pay or Alipay — carrying cash marks you as a tourist.
entries.follow_up: Convenience store vocabulary is practical and high-frequency. 热的还是冷的？(hot or cold?) and 需要袋子吗？(need a bag?) come up every time.

entries.title: Apartment Tour: Life in a Tier-3 City

segment.description: A young couple shows their spacious, affordable apartment in a smaller city — big kitchen, balcony garden, quiet streets.
segment.description_zh: 一对年轻人展示他们在小城市宽敞又便宜的公寓——大厨房、阳台花园、安静的街道。

where_to_find.primary: Bilibili: search '三线城市 房子 生活'

questions.q_zh: 三线城市的房子和大城市比有什么不同？
questions.q_en: How do tier-3 city apartments compare to big cities?
questions.explanation: You can see a young couple showing their spacious — the bigger and cheaper setting is clear from the opening shots.
安静 combines 安 (peace/safe) + 静 (quiet/still). You hear it in this segment, a young couple showing their spacious.

entries.cultural_note: China's tier system (一线/二线/三线) roughly maps to city size and development. Tier-3 cities are where most Chinese people actually live — slower pace, lower costs, tighter communities.
entries.follow_up: The tier-3 city lifestyle is the real China that tourists never see. These vlogs correct the distorted view that China = Shanghai + Beijing.

entries.title: Bullet Train Journey Vlog

segment.description: Boarding a 高铁 from Shanghai to Nanjing — buying tickets, finding seats, watching countryside fly past at 350km/h.
segment.description_zh: 从上海坐高铁到南京——买票、找座位、看窗外的风景飞速掠过。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '中国高铁 vlog'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '高铁 旅行 体验'

questions.q_zh: 高铁的速度大概是多少？
questions.q_en: About how fast does the bullet train go?
questions.explanation: In this 'Bullet Train Journey' segment, boarding a 高铁 from Shanghai to Nanjing. The answer follows from what you see and hear.
座位 means seat. Listen for it in this 'Bullet Train Journey' segment, boarding a 高铁 from Shanghai to Nanjing —.

entries.cultural_note: China's high-speed rail network is the world's largest — over 40,000 km. It has fundamentally changed how Chinese people think about distance. A 1,300km trip is now a 4.5-hour train ride.
entries.follow_up: Train station announcements are great listening practice. The phrase 列车即将到达 (the train is about to arrive at...) repeats at every stop.

entries.title: Chinese University Campus Tour

segment.description: A student walks through campus — dorm rooms, canteen, library, basketball courts, study rooms packed at midnight.
segment.description_zh: 一个学生在校园里走——宿舍、食堂、图书馆、篮球场、半夜还挤满人的自习室。

where_to_find.primary: Bilibili: search '大学校园 vlog 参观'

questions.explanation: You can see a student walking through campus — the the canteen setting is clear from the opening shots.
食堂 means canteen/dining hall. Listen for it in this 'University Campus Tour' segment, a student walking through campus.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese universities are self-contained campuses — students live in dorms, eat in canteens, and study in libraries until late. The 高考 (college entrance exam) that gets them there is the most important test of their lives.
entries.follow_up: University canteens serve some of the cheapest, most authentic food in China. If you ever visit, ask to eat there.

entries.title: How Chinese People Really Eat at Home

segment.description: A family cooks dinner together — three simple dishes, rice, soup. No chef tricks, just real home cooking.
segment.description_zh: 一家人一起做晚饭——三个简单的菜、米饭、汤。没有花哨手法，就是真正的家常菜。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '中国家常饭 真实 vlog'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '家常菜 做饭 日常'

questions.q_en: How many dishes did they make?
questions.explanation: As you watch a family cooks dinner together, count each one on screen to confirm three.
做饭 means cook. Listen for it in this 'Chinese Home Cooking' segment, a family cooks dinner together.

entries.cultural_note: Real Chinese home cooking is simpler than restaurant food — 2-3 dishes, rice, maybe soup. The phrase 家常便饭 (home-style simple meal) captures the philosophy: everyday food, nothing fancy.
entries.follow_up: The kitchen vocabulary in this video is among the most useful Chinese you can learn. 切 (cut), 炒 (stir-fry), 放盐 (add salt) — you'll use these.

entries.title: Beijing vs Shanghai: Two Cities, Two Worlds
entries.title_zh: 北京vs上海：两个城市，两个世界

segment.description: Side-by-side comparison: food, architecture, pace of life, dialect, culture. Two Chinas in two cities.
segment.description_zh: 并排对比：美食、建筑、生活节奏、方言、文化。两个城市，两个中国。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '北京vs上海 对比 vlog'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '北京 上海 区别'
search_terms: 北京 上海 对比

questions.q_zh: 视频对比了哪两个城市？
questions.q_en: Which two cities does the video compare?
questions.explanation: Pay attention to the delivery: side-by-side comparison: food, architecture, pace of life, dialect, culture. The tone and body language reveal beijing and shanghai.
区别 means difference. Listen for it in this 'Beijing vs Shanghai Comparison' segment, side-by-side comparison: food, architecture, pace of life.

entries.cultural_note: The Beijing-Shanghai rivalry is real. Beijing is political, historical, northern; Shanghai is commercial, cosmopolitan, southern. Chinese people identify strongly with their city's character.
entries.follow_up: Ask any Chinese person 你觉得北京好还是上海好？ and you'll get a passionate answer. It's a conversation starter that never fails.

entries.title: Day in the Life: Chinese Office Worker
entries.title_zh: 一天的生活：中国上班族

segment.description: A tech worker in Shenzhen: alarm at 7, subway, WeChat meetings, lunch delivery, overtime, late subway home.
segment.description_zh: 深圳科技公司员工的一天：7点闹钟、地铁、微信会议、外卖午餐、加班、赶末班地铁回家。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '中国上班族 一天 vlog'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '程序员 一天 vlog 深圳'

questions.q_en: How does this person eat lunch?
questions.explanation: Pay attention to the delivery: a tech worker in Shenzhen: alarm at 7, subway. The tone and body language reveal orders delivery.
外卖 means food delivery. Listen for it in this 'Office Worker Day in Life' segment, a tech worker in Shenzhen: alarm at 7.

entries.cultural_note: Food delivery (外卖) via Meituan or Ele.me has transformed Chinese work culture. Office workers order lunch to their desk daily — it's cheap, fast, and universal.
entries.follow_up: This vlog contains the vocabulary of modern Chinese daily life: 打卡 (clock in), 开会 (meeting), 末班车 (last train). Every word is high-frequency.

entries.title: How Chinese Cities Are Designed
entries.title_zh: 中国城市是怎么设计的

segment.description: Why Chinese cities have massive boulevards, gated compounds, and underground malls. Urban planning as ideology.
segment.description_zh: 为什么中国城市有宽阔大道、封闭小区和地下商场。城市规划即意识形态。

where_to_find.primary: Bilibili: search '中国城市规划 科普 为什么'
search_terms: 中国城市规划 科普

questions.q_zh: 中国城市的马路为什么这么宽？
questions.q_en: Why are Chinese city roads so wide?
questions.explanation: You can see why Chinese cities have massive boulevards, gated compounds — the urban planning ideology setting is clear from the opening shots.
小区 combines 小 (small) + 区 (area/district). You hear it in this segment, why Chinese cities have massive boulevards.

questions.q_zh: 这个视频是什么类型的？
questions.q_en: What type of video is this?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: why Chinese cities have massive boulevards, gated compounds. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm educational explainer.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese 小区 (gated residential compounds) are the basic unit of urban life. Each one has its own shops, playgrounds, and security guards. They're mini-cities within the city.
entries.follow_up: Understanding how Chinese cities are built helps you understand how Chinese society is organized. The 小区 system is a window into how institutions shape daily life.

entries.title: Let the Bullets Fly: The Train Robbery

segment.description: Bandits rob a horse-drawn train. The bandit leader Zhang decides to impersonate the new county governor. A dark comedy of power and pretense.
segment.description_zh: 土匪劫了一辆马拉火车。匪首张麻子决定冒充新上任的县长。一部关于权力和伪装的黑色喜剧。

where_to_find.primary: Apple TV or Google Play rental
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '让子弹飞 开头' (clips sometimes available)

questions.q_en: What does Zhang decide to do?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: bandits rob a horse-drawn train. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm impersonate the county governor.
土匪 means bandit. Listen for it in this 'Let the Bullets Fly' segment, bandits rob a horse-drawn train.

entries.cultural_note: 让子弹飞 is the most quotable Chinese film of the 21st century. Its lines about power, corruption, and the gap between appearance and reality are memed constantly online.
entries.follow_up: This film's dialogue is rapid, witty, and layered with political satire. Watch with Chinese subtitles on — it rewards re-watching.

entries.title: Dying to Survive: The Pharmacy Scene

segment.description: Cheng Yong meets a leukemia patient who can't afford medicine. The conversation about price, life, and what's fair.
segment.description_zh: 程勇遇到一个买不起药的白血病人。关于价格、生命和公平的对话。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '我不是药神 完整版' (often available)

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: cheng Yong meeting a leukemia patient who can't afford medicine. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm can't afford medicine.
买不起 means can't afford. Listen for it in this 'Dying to Survive' segment, cheng Yong meeting a leukemia patient who can't.

entries.cultural_note: This film changed Chinese law — it directly influenced the government to include more drugs in national health insurance. Cinema as institutional reform.
entries.follow_up: The 买不起 pattern (verb + 不 + complement) is one of the most useful grammar structures in Chinese. 听不懂 (can't understand), 吃不了 (can't eat it all).

entries.title: Eat Drink Man Woman: The Kitchen Symphony

segment.description: A retired chef prepares an elaborate Sunday dinner for his three daughters. No dialogue — just hands, woks, steam, and precision.
segment.description_zh: 退休大厨为三个女儿准备精致的周日晚餐。没有对话——只有双手、锅、蒸汽和精确。

where_to_find.primary: Apple TV or Google Play rental
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '饮食男女 开头 做饭' (opening sometimes available)

questions.q_en: Who is the old man cooking for?
questions.explanation: In this 'Eat Drink Man Woman' segment, a retired chef prepares an elaborate Sunday dinner. Listen for how his daughters is introduced.
厨师 means chef. Listen for it in this 'Eat Drink Man Woman' segment, a retired chef prepares an elaborate Sunday dinner.

entries.cultural_note: Ang Lee's opening sequence is one of cinema's greatest food scenes. The cooking IS the communication — in Chinese families, feeding people is how you say 'I love you.'
entries.follow_up: This is a Taiwanese Mandarin film — the accent is softer than mainland standard. The film's theme (food = love = family obligation) is deeply Chinese.

entries.title: The Piano in a Factory: Building the Piano

segment.description: Laid-off steelworkers decide to build a piano from scrap metal so a father can keep his daughter. Working class ingenuity and absurd beauty.
segment.description_zh: 下岗钢铁工人决定用废钢铁造一架钢琴，这样一个父亲就能留住女儿。工人阶级的聪明才智和荒诞之美。

where_to_find.primary: Apple TV or Amazon rental
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '钢的琴 电影' (clips available)

questions.q_zh: 工人们为什么要造钢琴？
questions.q_en: Why are the workers building a piano?

options.text: 为了帮一个父亲留住女儿
options.text_en: To help a father keep his daughter

questions.explanation: The context makes this clear: laid-off steelworkers decide to build a piano from scrap metal. That directly explains why to help a father keep.
工人 combines 工 (work) + 人 (person). You hear it in this segment, laid-off steelworkers decide to build a piano.

entries.cultural_note: This film captures the 下岗 (laid-off) era of the 1990s-2000s when millions of state factory workers lost their jobs. It's funny and heartbreaking and entirely real.
entries.follow_up: The northeast (东北) dialect in this film is distinctive — harder, more rhythmic, funnier than standard Mandarin. Dongbei humor is China's comedy capital.

entries.title: The Long Season: Something Happened at the Factory
entries.title_zh: 漫长的季节：工厂出事了

segment.description: An aging taxi driver in a crumbling industrial town flashes back to 1997, when he was a factory foreman and something terrible happened.
segment.description_zh: 一个衰败工业小城的出租车司机回忆1997年，那时他是工厂的工头，发生了可怕的事。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '漫长的季节 第一集'
search_terms: 漫长的季节 第一集

questions.q_en: What does the protagonist do now?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: an aging taxi driver in a crumbling industrial town flashes. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm taxi driver.
回忆 combines 回 (return) + 忆 (recall). You hear it in this segment, an aging taxi driver in a crumbling industrial.

questions.q_zh: 故事发生在什么样的地方？
questions.q_en: What kind of place does the story take place in?
questions.explanation: You can see an aging taxi driver in a crumbling industrial town flashes — the a declining industrial town setting is clear from the opening.

entries.cultural_note: 漫长的季节 is considered the best Chinese TV series of 2023. It's a northeast noir — slow, observational, and devastating. The industrial decay it depicts is real.
entries.follow_up: This show has the same energy as The Bad Kids but is set in a working-class world instead of a middle-class one. Watch the first three episodes and you'll be hooked.

entries.title: The Knockout: The Fish Stall Confrontation

segment.description: Gao Qiqiang, a timid fish seller, is bullied by local thugs. A cop intervenes. This small moment sets a 20-year transformation in motion.
segment.description_zh: 胆小的鱼贩高启强被地痞欺负。一个警察出面干预。这个小小的时刻启动了一场二十年的蜕变。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '狂飙 第一集 鱼摊'
search_terms: 狂飙 高启强 鱼摊

questions.q_zh: 高启强一开始做什么工作？
questions.q_en: What is Gao Qiqiang's job at the start?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Gao Qiqiang, a timid fish seller. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm selling fish.
欺负 means bully. Listen for it in this 'The Knockout' segment, Gao Qiqiang, a timid fish seller.

entries.cultural_note: 狂飙 broke Chinese streaming records in 2023. It traces how a small-town nobody becomes a crime boss over 20 years — a dark mirror of China's economic transformation.
entries.follow_up: The fish stall scene is the most famous first episode opening in recent Chinese TV. Everyone in China knows the line 你可以叫我强哥 (You can call me Brother Qiang).

entries.title: Infernal Affairs: The Rooftop Meeting

segment.description: The undercover cop and the mole meet secretly on a rooftop. Each man trapped in the other's identity. Who is the real fake?
segment.description_zh: 卧底警察和内鬼在天台秘密会面。两个人困在彼此的身份里。谁才是真正的假货？

where_to_find.primary: Apple TV or Google Play rental

questions.q_en: What is the two men's problem?

options.text: 他们困在彼此的身份里
options.text_en: They're trapped in each other's identity

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the undercover cop and the mole meet secretly. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm they're trapped in each other's identity.
卧底 means undercover. Listen for it in this 'Infernal Affairs' segment, the undercover cop and the mole meet secretly.

entries.cultural_note: Infernal Affairs is the film that Martin Scorsese remade as The Departed. Many consider the Cantonese/Mandarin original far superior — tighter, more philosophical, less violent.
entries.follow_up: This film uses both Cantonese and Mandarin. The Mandarin version is widely available. The rooftop scenes are some of the tensest in cinema.

entries.title: To Live: The Last Family Dinner

segment.description: The family eats together quietly. After decades of political upheaval, loss, and survival, the simple act of eating together is enough.
segment.description_zh: 一家人安静地吃饭。经历了几十年的政治动荡、失去和生存之后，能一起吃饭就够了。

where_to_find.primary: Apple TV or specialized film rental
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '活着 张艺谋 完整版' (sometimes available)

questions.q_en: What has this family been through?

options.text: 几十年的政治动荡和失去
options.text_en: Decades of political upheaval and loss

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the family eats together quietly. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm decades of political upheaval and loss.
活着 means to live/alive. Listen for it in this 'To Live' segment, the family eats together quietly.

entries.cultural_note: 活着 spans from the 1940s to the 1980s — civil war, land reform, the Great Leap Forward, the Cultural Revolution. It's the single best film for understanding modern Chinese history through one family's eyes.
entries.follow_up: This film is banned in mainland China. That fact itself teaches you something about the relationship between art and institutions in China.

entries.title: Three-Body Problem: The Countdown Begins

segment.description: A scientist sees a mysterious countdown only she can see. Physics is breaking down. The tension is intellectual, not physical.
segment.description_zh: 一个科学家看到只有她能看见的神秘倒计时。物理学在崩溃。紧张感是智识上的，不是身体上的。

where_to_find.primary: Netflix: search '三体' or 'Three-Body Problem'

questions.q_zh: 科学家看到了什么奇怪的东西？
questions.q_en: What strange thing does the scientist see?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a scientist sees a mysterious countdown only she can see. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm a countdown.
科学家 combines 学 (study/learn) + 家 (family/home). You hear it in this segment, a scientist sees a mysterious countdown only she.

entries.cultural_note: 刘慈欣's Three-Body Problem is the first Chinese novel to win the Hugo Award. It's national pride — Chinese sci-fi taken seriously on the world stage.
entries.follow_up: The Chinese TV adaptation is more faithful to the novel than the Netflix version. If you watch both, you get two different translation philosophies.

entries.title: I Can I BiBi: Should You Quit a Boring Job?
entries.title_zh: 奇葩说：无聊的工作该不该辞职？
entries.content_name_en: I Can I BiBi (Qi Pa Shuo)

segment.description: Contestants debate whether you should quit a boring but stable job. Arguments are funny, emotional, and rhetorically sharp.
segment.description_zh: 选手辩论无聊但稳定的工作该不该辞。论据搞笑、感人、修辞犀利。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '奇葩说 辞职 辩论'

options.text: 该不该辞掉无聊的工作
options.text_en: Whether to quit a boring job

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: contestants debate whether you should quit a boring. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm whether to quit a boring job.
稳定 means stable. Listen for it in this 'I Can I BiBi (Qi Pa Shuo)' segment, contestants debate whether you should quit a boring.

entries.cultural_note: 奇葩说 popularized debate as entertainment in China. Its format — funny but substantive arguments about life choices — created a new genre. The show made intellectualism cool for young audiences.
entries.follow_up: The debaters speak fast and use rhetoric strategies. Don't try to catch everything — focus on the argument structure. When you can follow the logic, you're at a high level.

entries.title: Rock & Roast: Yang Li on Gender Dynamics
entries.title_zh: 脱口秀大会：杨笠说男女关系

segment.description: Yang Li delivers her famous set about men's unearned confidence. Sharp, deadpan, and culturally explosive.
segment.description_zh: 杨笠表演她关于男性莫名自信的经典段子。犀利、冷面、文化炸弹。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '杨笠 脱口秀 男人'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '杨笠 脱口秀大会'

questions.q_en: What topic is Yang Li talking about?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Yang Li delivering her famous set about men's unearned confidence. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm men's confidence.
自信 means confidence. Listen for it in this 'Rock & Roast (Chinese Standup)' segment, Yang Li delivering her famous set about men's.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese standup (脱口秀) exploded in the 2020s. Yang Li's famous line '他明明那么普通，却那么自信' (he's so ordinary, yet so confident) became a national catchphrase.
entries.follow_up: Standup comedy is one of the hardest things to understand in any language. If you can follow Chinese standup, your listening comprehension is genuinely advanced.

entries.title: Round Table: Loneliness in Modern Cities

segment.description: Four intellectuals discuss why young Chinese urbanites feel so lonely despite being surrounded by millions. The conversation is thoughtful and unhurried.
segment.description_zh: 四位知识分子讨论为什么年轻的中国城市人在千万人中感到孤独。对话深思熟虑、不紧不慢。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '圆桌派 孤独'
where_to_find.alt: Youku: search '圆桌派 孤独 城市'
search_terms: 圆桌派 城市 年轻人

questions.q_zh: 他们讨论的核心问题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the core question they discuss?

options.text: 为什么城市里的年轻人感到孤独

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: four intellectuals discuss why young Chinese urbanites feel so lonely. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm why young urbanites feel lonely.
孤独 means lonely/loneliness. Listen for it in this 'Round Table (Yuan Zhuo Pai)' segment, four intellectuals discuss why young Chinese urbanites feel.

entries.cultural_note: The tension between collectivist culture and individual loneliness is one of modern China's defining contradictions. 圆桌派 is one of the few shows that engages with this honestly.
entries.follow_up: 圆桌派's tempo is perfect for advanced listening practice — the speakers are articulate but conversational, not scripted. It's the Chinese equivalent of a great podcast.

entries.title: Story FM: Why I Left My Hometown
entries.title_zh: 故事FM：我为什么离开家乡

segment.description: A woman from a small town tells her story of moving to Beijing — the gap between expectation and reality, the loneliness of reinvention.
segment.description_zh: 一个小镇女孩讲述她搬到北京的故事——期望与现实的差距，重新开始的孤独。

where_to_find.primary: Apple Podcasts / Spotify: search '故事FM'
where_to_find.alt: Ximalaya app: search '故事FM 离开家乡'

questions.q_zh: 讲故事的人从哪里搬到哪里？
questions.q_en: Where did the storyteller move from and to?

options.text_en: From a small town to Beijing

questions.explanation: You can see a woman from a small town tells her story — the from a small town to Beijing setting is clear.
家乡 means hometown. Listen for it in this 'Story FM Podcast' segment, a woman from a small town tells.

questions.q_zh: 搬到大城市后她感觉怎么样？
questions.q_en: How does she feel after moving to the big city?
questions.explanation: You can see a woman from a small town tells her story — the lonely setting is clear from the opening shots.

entries.cultural_note: 故事FM is China's best narrative podcast — real people telling their own stories without polish. The internal migration story (small town to big city) is the defining Chinese experience of the last 30 years.
entries.follow_up: Podcasts are the hardest listening medium — no visual cues, no subtitles. Start by listening once without pausing, then again with the transcript if available.

entries.title: Ritanpark Podcast: Modern Dating in China
entries.title_zh: 日谈公园：中国现代相亲

segment.description: The hosts discuss the absurdity of modern Chinese dating — parents posting résumés in parks, dating apps, the 'three haves' (house, car, savings).
segment.description_zh: 主持人讨论中国现代相亲的荒诞——父母在公园贴简历、交友软件、'三有'（有房有车有存款）。

where_to_find.primary: Apple Podcasts / Spotify: search '日谈公园'
where_to_find.alt: Ximalaya app: search '日谈公园 相亲'

questions.q_zh: 中国父母在公园做什么？
questions.q_en: What do Chinese parents do in parks?

options.text_en: Post matchmaking résumés for their kids

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the hosts discuss the absurdity of modern Chinese dating —. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm post matchmaking résumés.
相亲 means blind date/matchmaking. Listen for it in this 'Ritanpark Podcast' segment, the hosts discuss the absurdity of modern Chinese.

entries.cultural_note: Parent-organized matchmaking corners in public parks (相亲角) are real and thriving. Parents bring printouts of their adult children's stats — income, education, height, hukou — and negotiate with other parents.
entries.follow_up: 日谈公园 captures how Chinese millennials talk when they're being honest — casual, ironic, self-aware. The tone is very different from formal media.

entries.title: A Bite of China S2: On the Road
entries.title_zh: 舌尖上的中国第二季：脚步

segment.description: The opening episode of season 2 follows food traditions along migration routes. The narration is literary and the vocabulary spans geography, agriculture, and cultural identity.
segment.description_zh: 第二季第一集沿着迁徙路线追溯食物传统。解说词文学化，词汇涉及地理、农业和文化认同。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '舌尖上的中国 第二季 脚步'
search_terms: 舌尖上的中国 脚步

questions.q_en: What is the theme of this episode?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the opening episode of season 2 following food traditions along. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm food traditions along migration routes.
传承 means inheritance/passing down. Listen for it in this 'A Bite of China' segment, the opening episode of season 2 following food.

entries.cultural_note: 舌尖上的中国 was a cultural phenomenon — it made food documentaries into appointment television. The narration style (written by 李翔) set a new standard for documentary prose in China.
entries.follow_up: The narration is denser than conversational Mandarin. Try listening without subtitles first for the rhythm, then rewatch with Chinese subtitles to catch the literary vocabulary.

entries.title: Hexi Corridor: Zhang Qian's Mission to the West

segment.description: The first episode tells the story of Zhang Qian's diplomatic mission westward during the Han Dynasty. Cinematic visuals, orchestral score, and formal historical narration.
segment.description_zh: 第一集讲述汉代张骞出使西域的故事。电影级画面、交响乐配乐、正式的历史解说。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '河西走廊 第一集 使者'

options.text_en: A diplomatic mission to the west

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the first episode tells the story of Zhang Qian's diplomatic. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm a diplomatic mission.
使者 means envoy/messenger. Listen for it in this 'Hexi Corridor' segment, the first episode tells the story of Zhang.

entries.cultural_note: 河西走廊 is one of the most visually stunning Chinese documentaries ever made. Its score by Yakira (雅尼拉) was composed specifically for the series. The narration uses formal written Chinese, which is excellent exposure to the register gap between spoken and written Mandarin.
entries.follow_up: Watch this alongside a map. The geography makes the language concrete — when you hear 西域 (western regions), you can see where it is. Spatial vocabulary sticks better when it's grounded.

entries.title: Masters in the Forbidden City: The Clock Restorers
entries.title_zh: 我在故宫修文物：钟表修复

segment.description: Master craftsmen restore centuries-old clocks in quiet workshops inside the Forbidden City. They explain their techniques with calm precision. The vocabulary spans craftsmanship, history, and materials.
segment.description_zh: 修复师在故宫安静的工作室里修复百年钟表。他们平静而精确地解释技术。词汇涉及工艺、历史和材料。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '我在故宫修文物 钟表'
search_terms: 我在故宫修文物 纪录片

questions.explanation: You can see master craftsmen restore centuries-old clocks in quiet workshops inside — the inside the Forbidden City setting is clear from the opening shots.
文物 means cultural relic. Listen for it in this 'Masters in the Forbidden City' segment, master craftsmen restore centuries-old clocks in quiet workshops.

entries.cultural_note: This documentary went viral on Bilibili and made the Forbidden City restorers into unlikely internet celebrities. It sparked a wave of interest in traditional Chinese craftsmanship among young people.
entries.follow_up: The restorers speak slowly and precisely about their work. This is ideal for building technical vocabulary in a calm listening environment. Their speech is standard Mandarin with no regional accent.

entries.title: Nirvana in Fire: The Return of Mei Changsu

segment.description: Mei Changsu arrives in the capital after years of exile. He is frail but brilliant. The dialogue is formal, the scheming is layered, and every character watches every other character.
segment.description_zh: 梅长苏流亡多年后到达京城。他身体虚弱但才华横溢。对话正式，谋略层层叠叠，每个人都在观察每个人。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '琅琊榜 第一集'

questions.q_zh: 梅长苏来京城之前在做什么？
questions.q_en: What was Mei Changsu doing before coming to the capital?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Mei Changsu arriving in the capital after years of exile. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm living in exile.
谋略 means strategy/scheme. Listen for it in this 'Nirvana in Fire' segment, Mei Changsu arriving in the capital after years.

entries.cultural_note: 琅琊榜 is widely considered one of the best Chinese TV dramas ever made. Its depiction of court intrigue draws directly on historical patterns from multiple dynasties. The dialogue is a bridge between classical and modern Chinese — formal enough to stretch you, modern enough to follow.
entries.follow_up: The show rewards re-watching. On first pass, follow the emotions and relationships. On second pass, catch the political subtext. The vocabulary of power, loyalty, and sacrifice repeats constantly.

entries.title: Round Table: Growing Old in China

segment.description: Dou Wentao and guests discuss aging, elder care, and filial piety in a society that is aging faster than its institutions can adapt. The conversation is frank and culturally rich.
segment.description_zh: 窦文涛和嘉宾讨论老龄化、养老和孝道——在一个老龄化速度超过制度适应能力的社会里。对话坦诚且文化内涵丰富。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '圆桌派 老龄化' or '圆桌派 养老'

questions.q_zh: 中国面临什么社会问题？
questions.q_en: What social problem does China face?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Dou Wentao and guests discuss aging, elder care. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm population aging.
养老 means elder care. Listen for it in this 'Round Table (Yuan Zhuo Pai)' segment, Dou Wentao and guests discuss aging.

entries.cultural_note: China's one-child policy created a demographic cliff. A single child may be responsible for two parents and four grandparents — the '4-2-1' problem. This episode gets at a tension that shapes daily life for hundreds of millions.
entries.follow_up: The vocabulary of social issues (老龄化, 养老, 社会保障) recurs constantly in news and commentary. Mastering this register opens up an enormous amount of real-world Chinese media.

entries.title: Rise of the Great Powers: Portugal and the Age of Discovery

segment.description: The first episode examines how Portugal became a global naval power. The narration is formal and analytical, using the vocabulary of statecraft, economics, and geopolitics.
segment.description_zh: 第一集分析葡萄牙如何成为全球海上强国。解说正式而分析性强，使用治国、经济和地缘政治的词汇。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '大国崛起 第一集'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '大国崛起 纪录片'

questions.q_zh: 第一集讲的是哪个国家？
questions.q_en: Which country does the first episode cover?
questions.explanation: In this 'Rise of the Great Powers' segment, the first episode examines how Portugal became a global naval. The answer follows from what you see.
崛起 means rise (of a power). Listen for it in this 'Rise of the Great Powers' segment, the first episode examines how Portugal became.

entries.cultural_note: 大国崛起 was a CCTV production that implicitly asked: what can China learn from the rise and fall of other great powers? It was widely interpreted as a statement about China's own ambitions. The subtext is as important as the text.
