entries.follow_up: Each episode covers a different country (Portugal, Spain, Netherlands, Britain, France, Germany, Japan, Russia, America). Watch them in order to build a vocabulary of geopolitics that repeats and compounds.

entries.title: Lang Eye on Finance: The Real Estate Bubble

segment.description: Economist Lang Xianping analyzes China's property market with sharp commentary and data. His Mandarin is fast, precise, and full of financial terminology.
segment.description_zh: 经济学家郎咸平用犀利的评论和数据分析中国房地产市场。他的普通话快速、精确，充满金融术语。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '财经郎眼 房地产'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '财经郎眼 房价'
search_terms: 财经郎眼 房地产泡沫

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: economist Lang Xianping analyzing China's property market with sharp commentary. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the real estate market.

questions.q_zh: 泡沫在经济学中是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does 泡沫 mean in economics?
questions.explanation: 泡沫在经济学中 combines 经 (pass through/classic) + 济 (aid/economy) + 学 (study/learn). You hear it in this segment, economist Lang Xianping analyzing China's property market.

entries.cultural_note: Housing is the single most important economic issue in modern China. Most middle-class wealth is tied to property. Understanding 房地产 vocabulary is understanding what keeps Chinese families up at night.
entries.follow_up: Lang Xianping speaks at native speed with no concessions to learners. Use this as a speed benchmark. If you can follow 40%, you're doing well at HSK 7.

entries.title: Left and Right Podcast: Cold War Legacy in Asia
entries.title_zh: 忽左忽右：冷战在亚洲的遗产
entries.content_name_en: Left and Right (Hu Zuo Hu You)

segment.description: Host Cheng Yiduo interviews a historian about how Cold War-era decisions still shape East Asian geopolitics. The language is academic but accessible, with rich vocabulary on international relations.
segment.description_zh: 主持人程衣铎采访一位历史学家，讨论冷战时期的决策如何至今仍影响东亚地缘政治。语言学术但易懂，国际关系词汇丰富。

where_to_find.primary: Apple Podcasts / Spotify: search '忽左忽右'
where_to_find.alt: Ximalaya app: search '忽左忽右 冷战'

questions.q_zh: 他们讨论的主要话题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the main topic they discuss?

options.text_en: The Cold War's impact on East Asia

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: host Cheng Yiduo interviewing a historian about how Cold War-era. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the cold war's impact.

questions.q_en: What does 地缘政治 mean?
questions.explanation: 地缘政治 combines 政 (politics) + 治 (govern). You hear it in this segment, host Cheng Yiduo interviewing a historian about how.

entries.cultural_note: 忽左忽右 is one of China's most intellectually ambitious podcasts. It covers history, geopolitics, and culture at a level comparable to Western longform journalism. The host's interview style is precise and respectful.
entries.follow_up: This podcast is audio-only, which forces pure listening comprehension. Start with episodes on topics you already know in English — your background knowledge will scaffold the Chinese vocabulary.

entries.title: Story FM: Life on the Assembly Line

segment.description: A factory worker in Guangdong describes daily life on the assembly line — the monotony, the friendships, and what keeps people going. Raw, first-person narration with regional accent traces.
segment.description_zh: 一个在广东的工厂工人描述流水线上的日常生活——单调、友谊、以及支撑人们继续的动力。原始的第一人称叙述，带有轻微的地方口音。

where_to_find.primary: Apple Podcasts / Spotify: search '故事FM'
where_to_find.alt: Ximalaya app: search '故事FM 工厂'

questions.q_zh: 讲故事的人在哪里工作？
questions.explanation: You can see a factory worker in Guangdong describing daily life — the a factory assembly line setting is clear from the opening shots.
流水线 means assembly line. Listen for it in this 'Story FM Podcast' segment, a factory worker in Guangdong describing daily life.

entries.cultural_note: China's manufacturing workforce — roughly 100 million people — rarely gets to tell their own stories in mainstream media. 故事FM gives them a microphone. The language is working-class Mandarin, not the polished speech of talk shows.
entries.follow_up: The slight regional accents in Story FM episodes are valuable exposure. Real China doesn't sound like a textbook. Train your ear to understand Mandarin with Cantonese, Sichuan, or northeastern coloring.

entries.title: News 1+1: The Education Arms Race

segment.description: Bai Yansong analyzes the government's crackdown on private tutoring and the broader crisis of educational pressure on Chinese families. His delivery is measured and his analysis sharp.
segment.description_zh: 白岩松分析政府对校外培训的整治以及中国家庭面临的教育压力危机。他的表达沉稳，分析犀利。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '新闻1+1 教育 双减'

questions.q_zh: 白岩松在分析什么政策？
questions.q_en: What policy is Bai Yansong analyzing?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Bai Yansong analyzing the government's crackdown on private tutoring. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the crackdown on private tutoring.
内卷 combines 内 (inside) + 卷 (roll/curl). You hear it in this segment, Bai Yansong analyzing the government's crackdown on private.

entries.cultural_note: 内卷 (involution) became the defining buzzword of Chinese society in the 2020s — the feeling that everyone is competing harder for diminishing returns. Understanding this word unlocks a huge amount of contemporary Chinese discourse.
entries.follow_up: Bai Yansong is one of China's most respected TV journalists. His speaking pace and diction are a gold standard for formal spoken Mandarin. Shadowing his delivery is excellent pronunciation practice.

entries.title: Behind the Headlines: The Anxiety of the Chinese Middle Class
entries.content_name_en: Behind the Headlines with Wen Tao

segment.description: Dou Wentao and guests dissect the anxieties that define China's new middle class — education, property, status, and the fear of falling. Fast-paced intellectual conversation with cultural depth.
segment.description_zh: 窦文涛和嘉宾剖析定义中国新中产的焦虑——教育、房产、地位和下滑恐惧。节奏快、有知识深度的对话。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '锵锵三人行 中产'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '锵锵三人行 焦虑'
search_terms: 锵锵三人行 中产阶级

questions.q_zh: 中国中产阶级最大的焦虑是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the biggest anxiety of China's middle class?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Dou Wentao and guests dissect the anxieties that define China's. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm education, property, and social status.
焦虑 means anxiety. Listen for it in this 'Behind the Headlines with Wen Tao' segment, Dou Wentao and guests dissect the anxieties that.

entries.cultural_note: The phrase 中产焦虑 captures a defining mood of 2010s-2020s China. The middle class grew rapidly but lives in constant fear of losing ground. This anxiety drives everything from tutoring spending to property speculation.
entries.follow_up: 锵锵三人行 is unscripted. The guests interrupt, joke, and go on tangents — exactly how educated Chinese people actually talk. It trains you for real conversation, not textbook dialogue.

entries.title: Road to Revival: The Millennium Shift

segment.description: The opening episode covers the Opium Wars and China's forced opening to the West. The narration is patriotic but historically detailed, with dense political and diplomatic vocabulary.
segment.description_zh: 第一集讲述鸦片战争和中国被迫对外开放。解说带有爱国色彩但历史细节丰富，政治和外交词汇密集。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '复兴之路 第一集'
search_terms: 复兴之路 鸦片战争

questions.q_zh: 第一集的历史背景是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the historical context of the first episode?

options.text_en: The Opium Wars and forced opening

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the opening episode covering the Opium Wars. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the opium wars and forced opening.
屈辱 means humiliation. Listen for it in this 'Road to Revival' segment, the opening episode covering the Opium Wars.

entries.cultural_note: The 'century of humiliation' (百年屈辱) narrative — from the Opium Wars to the founding of the PRC — is the single most important frame for understanding modern Chinese nationalism. This documentary is a primary vehicle for that narrative.
entries.follow_up: Compare this documentary's framing with Western accounts of the same events. The vocabulary differences alone (屈辱 vs 'opening of China') reveal how language shapes historical understanding.

entries.title: YiXi Talk: Urban Villages and Informal Cities
entries.title_zh: 一席：城中村与非正式城市

segment.description: An urban planner gives a talk about 城中村 (urban villages) — informal settlements within Chinese megacities that house millions of migrants. The language is academic, the subject deeply human.
segment.description_zh: 一位城市规划师讲述城中村——中国大城市中容纳数百万流动人口的非正式定居点。语言学术化，主题却深具人文关怀。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '一席 城中村'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '一席 城市规划'

questions.q_zh: 城中村住的主要是什么人？
questions.q_en: Who mainly lives in urban villages?

options.text: 流动人口和外来务工人员

questions.explanation: In this 'YiXi (One Seat)' segment, an urban planner gives a talk about 城中村 (urban villages). Listen for how migrants and outside workers is introduced.
拆迁 combines 拆 (tear down) + 迁 (move/relocate). You hear it in this segment, an urban planner gives a talk about 城中村.

entries.cultural_note: 城中村 are one of the most fascinating features of Chinese urbanization — villages that were swallowed by expanding cities but retained their own governance, architecture, and social structures. They're being demolished at speed, making this talk a time capsule.
entries.follow_up: 一席 talks are China's equivalent of TED talks but more academic and less performative. The speakers assume an educated audience. Perfect for building academic register comprehension.

entries.title: YiXi Talk: The Ethics of Documentary Filmmaking

segment.description: A documentary filmmaker discusses the ethical dilemmas of filming real people in difficult circumstances — consent, power dynamics, and the camera's gaze. The vocabulary spans philosophy, media theory, and human rights.
segment.description_zh: 一位纪录片导演讨论拍摄处于困境中的真实人物的伦理困境——知情同意、权力关系和镜头的凝视。词汇涵盖哲学、媒体理论和人权。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '一席 纪录片 伦理'

questions.q_zh: 演讲者讨论的核心问题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the core issue the speaker discusses?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a documentary filmmaker discussing the ethical dilemmas of filming real. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm ethical dilemmas of documentary filmmaking.
伦理 means ethics. Listen for it in this 'YiXi (One Seat)' segment, a documentary filmmaker discussing the ethical dilemmas.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese independent documentary has a rich tradition — from Wu Wenguang's pioneering work in the 1990s to today's filmmakers working at the margins. The ethical questions raised here are universal but gain particular weight in a context where subjects may face political risk from visibility.
entries.follow_up: This talk builds vocabulary for discussing abstract concepts in Chinese — a crucial skill at HSK 8. Practice summarizing the speaker's arguments in your own words after watching.

entries.title: Vistopia: Why Law Matters in Everyday Life
entries.title_zh: 看理想：法律为什么和日常生活有关

segment.description: A legal scholar explains how Chinese law actually works in practice — the gap between written statutes and lived reality. The language is precise and formal, with legal and bureaucratic terminology.
segment.description_zh: 一位法学学者解释中国法律在实践中如何运作——成文法和现实之间的差距。语言精确正式，充满法律和行政术语。

where_to_find.primary: 看理想 (Vistopia) app: search '法律'
where_to_find.alt: Apple Podcasts: search '看理想 法律'

questions.q_zh: 演讲者讨论的主要问题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the main issue the speaker discusses?

options.text: 成文法与现实之间的差距
options.text_en: The gap between written law and reality

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a legal scholar explaining how Chinese law actually works. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the gap between written law.
维权 means defend rights. Listen for it in this 'Vistopia' segment, a legal scholar explaining how Chinese law actually.

entries.cultural_note: 维权 (rights defense) is a loaded term in Chinese discourse. Understanding the legal vocabulary of everyday life — contracts, labor disputes, property rights — reveals how Chinese society actually functions at ground level.
entries.follow_up: Legal Chinese is its own register. Once you can parse it, you unlock access to court documents, legal commentary, and policy analysis — a huge body of material that most learners never reach.

entries.title: Luo Xiang: The Philosophy Behind Criminal Law

segment.description: Law professor Luo Xiang uses vivid hypothetical scenarios to explain criminal law principles. His style is uniquely engaging — he combines legal precision with philosophical depth and dry humor.
segment.description_zh: 法学教授罗翔用生动的假设情景解释刑法原则。他的风格独特——将法律精确性与哲学深度和冷幽默结合起来。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '罗翔 刑法'

questions.q_zh: 罗翔用什么方法来解释法律？
questions.q_en: What method does Luo Xiang use to explain law?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: law professor Luo Xiang uses vivid hypothetical scenarios to explain. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm hypothetical scenarios.

questions.q_en: What does 正当防卫 mean?
questions.explanation: 正当防卫 means justifiable defense. Listen for it in this 'Luo Xiang on Criminal Law' segment, law professor Luo Xiang uses vivid hypothetical scenarios.

entries.cultural_note: Luo Xiang became the most popular professor on Chinese internet — his Bilibili channel gained millions of subscribers. He made legal thinking accessible and entertaining. His character '张三' (a hypothetical criminal in every scenario) became a meme.
entries.follow_up: Luo Xiang's lectures are a masterclass in Chinese public speaking. His pacing, humor, and rhetorical structure are worth studying separately from the legal content. Shadow his delivery patterns.

entries.title: YiXi Talk: Mental Health in Chinese Society
entries.title_zh: 一席：中国社会的心理健康

segment.description: A psychologist discusses the stigma around mental health in China, the generational gap in understanding depression, and how language itself shapes what people can express about their suffering.
segment.description_zh: 一位心理学家讨论中国社会对心理健康的污名化、代际间对抑郁症理解的差异，以及语言本身如何塑造人们表达痛苦的方式。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '一席 心理健康' or '一席 抑郁'

questions.q_zh: 演讲者认为语言对心理健康有什么影响？
questions.q_en: What does the speaker think language does to mental health?

options.text: 语言塑造人们表达痛苦的方式
options.text_en: Language shapes how people express suffering

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a psychologist discussing the stigma around mental health in China. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm language shapes how people express.
污名化 means stigmatization. Listen for it in this 'YiXi (One Seat)' segment, a psychologist discussing the stigma around mental health.

entries.cultural_note: Mental health vocabulary is relatively new in everyday Chinese. Older generations may say '想不开' (can't think through it) where younger people say '抑郁' (depression). The vocabulary shift itself tracks a cultural transformation.
entries.follow_up: This talk is HSK 8 because the vocabulary is specialized (psychology, sociology) and the arguments are abstract. But the subject matter is deeply relevant to understanding contemporary Chinese life.

entries.title: Ming Dynasty 1566: The Emperor's Taoist Ritual
entries.title_zh: 大明王朝1566：皇帝的道教仪式

segment.description: The Jiajing Emperor performs a Taoist ritual while his ministers wait. The scene is dense with classical Chinese vocabulary, religious terminology, and the language of imperial power expressed through ritual silence.
segment.description_zh: 嘉靖帝举行道教仪式，大臣们在外等候。场景中充满文言词汇、宗教术语以及通过仪式性沉默表达的皇权语言。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '大明王朝1566 嘉靖 道教'

questions.q_en: What is the Jiajing Emperor doing?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the Jiajing Emperor performs a Taoist ritual while his ministers. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm performing a taoist ritual.
天命 means mandate of heaven. Listen for it in this 'Ming Dynasty 1566' segment, the Jiajing Emperor performs a Taoist ritual while.

entries.cultural_note: The Jiajing Emperor was famously obsessed with Taoist immortality rituals, often neglecting state affairs. This show uses that historical fact to explore how power operates through absence — the emperor's silence forces everyone else to guess his intentions.
entries.follow_up: This scene requires you to read meaning from what is NOT said. At HSK 8, you should be building sensitivity to implication, euphemism, and silence as communication strategies in Chinese.

entries.title: YiXi Talk: Inside China's Investigative Journalism
entries.title_zh: 一席：中国调查记者的故事

segment.description: An investigative journalist recounts major stories they pursued — the obstacles, the personal cost, and the shrinking space for this kind of work. The language is measured, the implications enormous.
segment.description_zh: 一位调查记者讲述他们追踪的重大报道——障碍、个人代价以及这类工作日益缩小的空间。语言克制，含义深远。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '一席 调查记者' or '一席 新闻'

questions.q_zh: 调查记者面临的最大挑战是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the biggest challenge facing investigative journalists?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: an investigative journalist recounts major stories they pursued. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm shrinking space for their work.
真相 means truth. Listen for it in this 'YiXi (One Seat)' segment, an investigative journalist recounts major stories they pursued.

entries.cultural_note: China had a golden age of investigative journalism in the 2000s. Understanding what happened to it — and the vocabulary used to discuss press freedom (新闻自由) and media supervision (舆论监督) — is essential cultural literacy.
entries.follow_up: Notice how the speaker navigates sensitive topics using careful word choices. At HSK 8, learning to read between the lines of Chinese public discourse is as important as expanding vocabulary.

entries.title: White Deer Plain: The Wheat Harvest

segment.description: The village gathers for the wheat harvest. Clan patriarch Bai Jiaxuan oversees the work. The dialogue is literary, steeped in Shaanxi dialect coloring, and rich with agricultural and clan vocabulary.
segment.description_zh: 全村人聚集收麦。族长白嘉轩监督劳作。对话文学化，带有陕西方言色彩，充满农业和宗族词汇。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '白鹿原 电视剧 第三集'

questions.q_zh: 白嘉轩在村里的地位是什么？
questions.q_en: What is Bai Jiaxuan's position in the village?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: the village gathering for the wheat harvest. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm clan patriarch.
庄稼 means crops. Listen for it in this 'White Deer Plain' segment, the village gathering for the wheat harvest.

entries.cultural_note: 白鹿原 (based on Chen Zhongshi's novel) is the great novel of rural China's transformation across the 20th century. The TV adaptation preserves the literary quality of the dialogue. It's rural Mandarin at its richest.
entries.follow_up: The Shaanxi dialect coloring adds an extra layer of difficulty. Don't worry about catching regional pronunciation — focus on the narrative structure and the vocabulary of land, kinship, and power that drives the story.

entries.title: Reader: Reciting Classical Chinese Prose

segment.description: A guest reads aloud from a classical Chinese text while host Dong Qing provides modern context. The segment bridges classical and modern Mandarin, with vocabulary that spans centuries.
segment.description_zh: 一位嘉宾朗诵古文，主持人董卿提供现代语境。这个环节架起古典与现代普通话之间的桥梁，词汇跨越数个世纪。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '朗读者 古文' or '朗读者 董卿'

questions.q_zh: 这个节目的形式是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the format of this show?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a guest reading aloud from a classical Chinese text while. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm guests recite classic texts.
意境 combines 意 (meaning/intention) + 境 (boundary/environment). You hear it in this segment, a guest reading aloud from a classical Chinese.

entries.cultural_note: 朗读者 was a cultural phenomenon — it made reading aloud fashionable again. The show's success reflects a deep cultural attachment to the sounds of Chinese language. Classical Chinese is not dead; it lives in idioms, formal writing, and moments like these.
entries.follow_up: Even if you understand only 30% of the classical passages, the experience of hearing them read beautifully trains your ear for the rhythm of formal Chinese. That rhythm carries into modern formal speech.

entries.title: Round Table: How Chinese Culture Talks About Death
entries.title_zh: 圆桌派：中国文化如何谈论死亡

segment.description: Dou Wentao and guests explore how Confucianism, Taoism, and Buddhism each approach mortality — and how modern Chinese people navigate between these frameworks. The vocabulary spans philosophy, religion, and existential reflection.
segment.description_zh: 窦文涛和嘉宾探讨儒释道各自如何看待死亡——以及现代中国人如何在这些框架之间穿行。词汇涵盖哲学、宗教和存在主义反思。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '圆桌派 死亡' or '圆桌派 生死'
where_to_find.alt: Youku: search '圆桌派 生死观'

questions.q_zh: 他们讨论了哪些思想体系？
questions.q_en: Which thought systems do they discuss?
questions.explanation: In this 'Round Table (Yuan Zhuo Pai)' segment, Dou Wentao and guests explore how Confucianism, Taoism. The answer follows from what you see and hear.
超脱 means transcendence/detachment. Listen for it in this 'Round Table (Yuan Zhuo Pai)' segment, Dou Wentao and guests explore how Confucianism.

entries.cultural_note: Confucius famously said '未知生，焉知死' (How can you understand death if you don't yet understand life?). This avoidance of death-talk still shapes Chinese culture. This episode is rare in engaging the topic directly.
entries.follow_up: This is philosophical Chinese at its most demanding. The three-way comparison (Confucian/Taoist/Buddhist) requires vocabulary from three separate intellectual traditions. Keep a vocabulary notebook specifically for this episode.

entries.title: The Thirteen Invitations: Xu Zhiyuan Interviews Himself
entries.title_zh: 十三邀：许知远采访自己

segment.description: In a special episode, Xu Zhiyuan reflects on his own intellectual journey — his relationship with Chinese modernity, his discomfort with the present, and the role of the public intellectual. The language is deeply literary and introspective.
segment.description_zh: 特别节目中，许知远反思自己的知识分子之路——他与中国现代性的关系、对当下的不适、以及公共知识分子的角色。语言高度文学化，充满内省。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '十三邀 许知远 反思'
where_to_find.alt: Tencent Video: search '十三邀 特别节目'

questions.q_en: What is Xu Zhiyuan reflecting on?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: in a special episode, Xu Zhiyuan reflects on his own. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm his own intellectual journey.
现代性 means modernity. Listen for it in this 'The Thirteen Invitations' segment, in a special episode.

entries.cultural_note: Xu Zhiyuan is one of the most polarizing figures in Chinese intellectual life. Some see him as a genuine thinker; others see him as performatively nostalgic. Understanding why he generates such strong reactions is itself a lesson in Chinese cultural politics.
entries.follow_up: This is arguably the most linguistically demanding content in the entire media catalog. If you can follow Xu Zhiyuan's literary Mandarin, you can follow anything. Treat it as an aspirational benchmark.

entries.title: Still Life: The Three Gorges Landscape

segment.description: A migrant worker searches for his wife in a town being demolished for the Three Gorges Dam. The dialogue is sparse and in Shanxi dialect-inflected Mandarin. The landscape does most of the talking.
segment.description_zh: 一个外来务工人员在因三峡大坝即将拆除的小城中寻找妻子。对话稀少，带山西方言色彩的普通话。风景承担了大部分叙事。

where_to_find.primary: Criterion Channel or art-house rental platforms
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '三峡好人 贾樟柯' (clips sometimes available)

questions.q_zh: 这个小城为什么要被拆除？
questions.q_en: Why is the town being demolished?

options.text_en: Construction of the Three Gorges Dam

questions.explanation: The context makes this clear: a migrant worker searching for his wife in a town. That directly explains why construction of the three gorges dam.
务工 means migrant labor. Listen for it in this 'Still Life' segment, a migrant worker searching for his wife.

entries.cultural_note: Jia Zhangke is China's most important living filmmaker. His work documents the human cost of modernization — the people left behind by progress. 三峡好人 won the Golden Lion at Venice and remains his masterpiece.
entries.follow_up: Jia Zhangke's films are dialogue-light. The language challenge is not vocabulary density but comprehension of sparse, dialect-colored speech in context. Train your ear for meaning beyond the words.

entries.title: In the Mood for Love: The Corridor Scene

segment.description: Chow Mo-wan and Su Li-zhen keep meeting in the narrow corridor of their apartment building. They discuss their spouses' affair with exquisite restraint. Every word carries the weight of what cannot be said.
segment.description_zh: 周慕云和苏丽珍在公寓楼的狭窄走廊里反复相遇。他们以极度克制的方式讨论各自配偶的婚外情。每个字都承载着不能说出口的重量。

where_to_find.primary: Criterion Channel or Apple TV rental
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '花样年华 Wong Kar-wai' (clips sometimes available)

questions.q_en: What are the two protagonists discussing?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: chow Mo-wan and Su Li-zhen keep meeting in the narrow. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm their spouses' affair.
克制 means restraint/self-control. Listen for it in this 'In the Mood for Love' segment, chow Mo-wan and Su Li-zhen keep meeting.

entries.cultural_note: Wong Kar-wai's masterpiece is about desire expressed through restraint. The Mandarin and Cantonese dialogue is minimal but precisely chosen — every word does double duty. The film teaches you that in Chinese communication, what matters most is often what remains unsaid.
entries.follow_up: The film's language is HSK 9 not because of vocabulary difficulty but because of pragmatic complexity — understanding implication, face management, and emotional indirection. This is the final frontier of language learning.

entries.title: Vistopia: Reading Zhuangzi in the Modern World
entries.title_zh: 看理想：在现代世界读庄子

segment.description: A philosophy professor close-reads passages from the Zhuangzi, connecting Warring States thought to contemporary anxieties. The language alternates between classical quotation and modern analysis.
segment.description_zh: 一位哲学教授精读《庄子》段落，将战国思想与当代焦虑联系起来。语言在古典引文和现代分析之间交替。

where_to_find.primary: 看理想 (Vistopia) app: search '庄子'
where_to_find.alt: Apple Podcasts: search '看理想 庄子'

questions.q_en: Which classic is the professor analyzing?
questions.explanation: In this 'Vistopia' segment, a philosophy professor close-reading passages from the Zhuangzi. The answer follows from what you see and hear.
无为 means non-action/effortless action. Listen for it in this 'Vistopia' segment, a philosophy professor close-reading passages from the Zhuangzi.

entries.cultural_note: The Zhuangzi is arguably the most beautiful text in the Chinese language. Its parables (the butterfly dream, the useless tree, Cook Ding's ox) are still referenced constantly in modern Chinese. Understanding these references is a marker of deep cultural literacy.
entries.follow_up: Classical Chinese is the deepest layer of Mandarin. You don't need to read it fluently, but recognizing classical structures when they appear in modern speech marks the difference between fluency and mastery.

entries.title: Kaili Blues: The Long Take Through Time
entries.title_zh: 路边野餐：穿越时间的长镜头

segment.description: A 40-minute continuous take follows the protagonist through a small Guizhou town where past, present, and future seem to merge. The dialogue is in Guizhou-inflected Mandarin mixed with Miao-influenced speech. Time and memory dissolve.
segment.description_zh: 一个40分钟的长镜头跟随主角穿过贵州小城，过去、现在和未来似乎融为一体。对话使用带贵州口音的普通话，夹杂苗族影响的表达。时间与记忆溶解。

where_to_find.primary: MUBI or art-house rental platforms
where_to_find.alt: YouTube: search '路边野餐 毕赣' (clips sometimes available)

questions.q_zh: 这部电影的拍摄手法有什么特别之处？
questions.q_en: What is special about this film's technique?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a 40-minute continuous take following the protagonist through a small. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm an extremely long continuous take.
迷失 means lost/disoriented. Listen for it in this 'Kaili Blues' segment, a 40-minute continuous take following the protagonist through.

entries.cultural_note: Bi Gan is the most exciting young Chinese filmmaker. He grew up in Kaili, Guizhou — one of China's poorest regions — and makes films that transform rural poverty into dreamlike poetry. His work represents a new wave of Chinese cinema rooted in specific local identity.
entries.follow_up: This is among the most challenging listening material in the catalog. The Guizhou accent, sparse dialogue, and non-linear narrative all work against easy comprehension. Use it as a sensory experience first, a language exercise second. Let the sounds wash over you.

entries.title: Behind the Headlines: Is Literature Still Relevant?
entries.title_zh: 锵锵三人行：文学还有意义吗？
entries.content_name_en: Behind the Headlines with Wen Tao

segment.description: Dou Wentao, a novelist, and a literary critic debate whether literature matters in the age of short video. The conversation ranges from Lu Xun to TikTok, mixing classical references with internet slang in the same breath.
segment.description_zh: 窦文涛、一位小说家和一位文学评论家讨论在短视频时代文学是否还有意义。对话从鲁迅谈到TikTok，在同一句话中混合古典典故和网络用语。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '锵锵三人行 文学'
where_to_find.alt: Bilibili: search '锵锵三人行 文学 意义'

questions.q_zh: 他们讨论文学和什么之间的关系？
questions.q_en: What relationship with literature do they discuss?
questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: Dou Wentao, a novelist, and a literary critic debate whether. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm the age of short video.
碎片化 means fragmentation. Listen for it in this 'Behind the Headlines with Wen Tao' segment, Dou Wentao, a novelist.

entries.cultural_note: The tension between 'deep reading' (深度阅读) and 'fragmented reading' (碎片化阅读) is a major cultural debate in China. This episode captures the anxiety of a literary culture encountering the smartphone era.
entries.follow_up: This episode is HSK 9 because it requires you to process register-switching in real time — the speakers move between classical allusion, formal analysis, colloquial speech, and internet slang within single sentences. This is how educated Chinese people actually talk.

entries.title: YiXi Talk: The Language of Chinese Political Discourse
entries.title_zh: 一席：中国政治话语的语言

segment.description: A linguist analyzes the specific vocabulary, sentence structures, and rhetorical patterns of official Chinese political language — how meaning is constructed, deflected, and encoded. The talk itself demonstrates mastery of multiple registers.
segment.description_zh: 一位语言学家分析中国官方政治语言的特定词汇、句式和修辞模式——意义如何被构建、转移和编码。演讲本身展示了对多种语域的掌握。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '一席 政治语言' or '一席 官方话语'

questions.explanation: Watch the segment closely: a linguist analyzing the specific vocabulary, sentence structures. Both the visuals and dialogue confirm patterns of official political language.
修辞 combines 修 (repair/cultivate) + 辞 (words/speech). You hear it in this segment, a linguist analyzing the specific vocabulary.

entries.cultural_note: Understanding how Chinese political language works — its euphemisms, its formulaic structures, its deliberate ambiguities — is the final key to full comprehension of Chinese media. When you can decode 人民日报 headlines, you have arrived.
entries.follow_up: This is meta-linguistic content: Chinese about Chinese. It requires you to think about language as a system, not just use it. This kind of metalinguistic awareness is what separates HSK 9 from everything below it.

entries.title: My Own Swordsman: Welcome to Tongfu Inn
entries.title_zh: 武林外传：同福客栈开张

segment.description: The staff of Tongfu Inn are introduced through a series of comedic misunderstandings. Each character enters with a running gag that defines them for the entire series.
segment.description_zh: 同福客栈的伙计们通过一系列误会登场，每个角色都带着贯穿全剧的喜剧标签出场。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '武林外传 第一集'

questions.q_en: Who is the innkeeper of Tongfu Inn?
questions.explanation: The innkeeper is 佟大娘 (Boss Tong), a widow who inherited the inn. The show’s humor partly comes from her trying to run a business with this chaotic staff.
客栈 combines 客 (guest) + 栈 (lodge/shelter). In wuxia fiction, the inn is the classic gathering place where strangers meet and trouble starts.

entries.cultural_note: 武林外传 is often called China’s answer to Friends-meets-wuxia. It parodies martial arts tropes by setting a sitcom inside a jianghu inn. The humor is heavily referential and pun-driven.
entries.follow_up: Watch the first 3 episodes to lock in the character dynamics. The comedy builds through callbacks, so isolated clips lose a lot.

entries.title: My Own Swordsman: The Inn's Talent Competition
entries.title_zh: 武林外传：客栈才艺大赛

segment.description: The inn staff compete in an absurd talent show. Each performance parodies a different genre. The judging descends into chaos as personal grudges take over.
segment.description_zh: 客栈伙计们举行才艺比赛，每个表演都在恶搞不同类型。评分环节因为私人恩怨变成了混战。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '武林外传 第七集'

questions.q_zh: 才艺大赛最后怎么样了？
questions.q_en: What happens to the talent competition in the end?
questions.explanation: The competition is a vehicle for character comedy. The actual winner doesn’t matter — the ensemble dynamics take over, which is the show’s signature move.
比赛 combines 比 (compare) + 赛 (contest). You’ll hear this in sports, reality shows, and any competitive context.

entries.cultural_note: The talent show episode is a fan favorite because it showcases the ensemble format. Each character’s performance reflects their personality type: the fighter does something violent, the scholar recites poetry badly, the accountant tries to monetize it.
entries.follow_up: After this episode, try listing each character’s “type” in Chinese. The show teaches you personality vocabulary through repetition.

entries.title: I Love My Family: Grandpa's Retirement Crisis
entries.title_zh: 我爱我家：爷爷退休的烦恼

segment.description: Retired cadre Old Fu struggles to adjust to life without authority. His family tries to keep him busy, but he keeps treating the household like a government office.
segment.description_zh: 退休干部老傅不适应没有权力的生活。家人试着让他忙起来，但他把家里当成了机关。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '我爱我家 第一集'
search_terms: I Love My Family Chinese sitcom

questions.q_zh: 老傅退休后有什么问题？
questions.q_en: What is Old Fu's problem after retirement?

options.text_en: He can't adjust to having no authority

questions.explanation: The comedy comes from a retired bureaucrat who can’t stop being a bureaucrat. He holds family meetings with agendas and takes attendance at dinner.
退休 combines 退 (withdraw) + 休 (rest). It specifically means retirement from work, not just taking a break.

entries.cultural_note: Written by Liang Zuo (梁左) and directed by Ying Da (英达), this was China’s first true sitcom. The retired-cadre premise is genius satire — it lets you laugh at bureaucratic habits without directly criticizing the system.
entries.follow_up: Notice how the laugh track works differently from American sitcoms. The audience was live, and some of the biggest laughs come from political double meanings that only a 1990s Beijing audience would catch.

entries.title: I Love My Family: Jia Zhi's Get-Rich Scheme
entries.title_zh: 我爱我家：贾志国的发财梦

segment.description: Middle son Jia Zhiguo announces a plan to get rich through a dubious business venture. The family reacts with a mix of mockery and genuine concern. His father delivers a withering monologue about honest work.
segment.description_zh: 二儿子贾志国宣布了一个不靠谱的发财计划。家人又嘲笑又担心。父亲发表了一段关于踏实工作的狠话。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '我爱我家 贾志国 做生意'
search_terms: I Love My Family business episode

questions.q_zh: 父亲觉得儿子应该怎么做？
questions.q_en: What does the father think his son should do?
questions.explanation: The father represents the old-guard work ethic. His monologue is both funny and genuinely moving — a hallmark of the show’s ability to mix comedy with real feeling.
踏实 literally means “stepping firmly.” It’s one of the highest compliments in Chinese work culture — the opposite of someone who chases shortcuts.

entries.cultural_note: The 1990s get-rich-quick fever (下海热) is a recurring target in this show. Millions of Chinese left stable state jobs to start businesses. The comedy captures both the excitement and the absurdity of that era.
entries.follow_up: Listen for the word 下海 (literally “go into the sea”) — it means to leave a stable job and start a business. It’s 1990s slang that older Chinese people still use.

entries.title: Rock & Roast S4: House Prices and Hopelessness
entries.title_zh: 脱口秀大会：房价与绝望

segment.description: A comedian does a tight seven minutes on the impossibility of buying an apartment in a Chinese city. The material is structurally sharp: setups about salary, punchlines about square meters, callbacks to his parents’ generation buying houses for pocket change.
segment.description_zh: 一位脱口秀演员用七分钟讲在中国城市买房的不可能。结构很紧：工资是铺垫，平方米是包袱，父母那代人的房价是回扣。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '脱口秀大会 房价'
search_terms: 脱口秀大会 买房 段子

questions.q_zh: 演员说买房最大的问题是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the comedian say is the biggest problem with buying a house?
questions.explanation: The entire set is built around the gap between salary and housing cost. The comedian uses specific numbers for effect, which is a hallmark of Chinese standup’s data-driven humor style.
贷款 combines 贷 (lend/borrow) + 款 (funds). In daily Chinese conversation, it almost always means mortgage, because that’s the loan everyone talks about.

entries.cultural_note: Chinese standup (脱口秀) is a young art form, only mainstream since ~2017. Unlike American standup’s confessional style, Chinese standup tends toward observational humor about shared social pressures: housing, 996 work culture, marriage expectations.
entries.follow_up: Watch 3-4 different comedians’ sets on the same topic. The vocabulary repeats, but each comedian’s angle is different. Great for building topic-specific fluency.

entries.title: Round Table: The Tyranny of Face Culture
entries.title_zh: 圆桌派：面子文化的暴政

segment.description: Dou Wentao leads a discussion on face culture (面子) with three guests. The conversation moves from wedding gift inflation to office politics to the philosophical question of whether face is a social contract or a prison. Dou’s signature move: asking a sharp question, then undercutting his own seriousness with a joke.
segment.description_zh: 窦文涛带领三位嘉宾讨论面子文化。从婚礼红包通货膨胀到职场政治，再到哲学问题：面子是社会契约还是监狱。窦文涛的招牌动作：问一个尖锐问题，然后用笑话拆解自己的严肃。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '圆桌派 面子'
search_terms: Round Table face culture Dou Wentao

questions.q_zh: 讨论中最核心的问题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the central question in the discussion?

options.text_en: How much wedding money to give
Whether face is a contract or a prison
Whether Chinese people are too polite

questions.explanation: The show’s strength is escalating from concrete examples (wedding red envelopes) to abstract questions (is face a social lubricant or a form of control?). Dou Wentao steers this escalation deliberately.

questions.q_zh: 面子在这里是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does 面子 mean in this context?
questions.explanation: 面子 literally means “face” but in social contexts it means prestige, dignity, reputation. Losing face (丢面子) or giving face (给面子) are fundamental social mechanics in Chinese culture.

entries.cultural_note: 圆桌派’s format — four people, tea, no studio audience — creates space for the kind of intellectual play that doesn’t survive on louder shows. Dou Wentao is a master of the sharp-but-warm question.
entries.follow_up: Pay attention to how guests disagree. Chinese intellectual discussion has its own rhetoric — you’ll hear “你说的有道理，但是” (you have a point, but) as the polite disagreement formula.

entries.title: The Thirteen Invitations: Cai Guoqiang on Gunpowder and Home
entries.title_zh: 十三邀：蔡国强谈火药与故乡

segment.description: Xu Zhiyuan interviews artist Cai Guoqiang in his studio. They discuss why he uses gunpowder as his medium, what it means to be a Chinese artist abroad, and whether art can be honest about politics without becoming propaganda. Cai is warm but precise; Xu pushes gently on contradictions.
segment.description_zh: 许知远在工作室采访艺术家蔡国强。讨论为什么用火药创作，在海外做中国艺术家的意义，以及艺术能否诚实地谈政治而不变成宣传。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '十三邀 蔡国强'
search_terms: 十三邀 蔡国强 火药

questions.q_zh: 蔡国强用什么材料创作？
questions.q_en: What material does Cai Guoqiang use to create art?
questions.explanation: Cai Guoqiang is famous for gunpowder art — controlled explosions on canvas. The interview explores how this connects to Chinese invention of gunpowder and to destruction as creation.
故乡 combines 故 (old/former) + 乡 (hometown/village). It carries more emotional weight than 老家 — it’s the literary, nostalgic word for the place you came from.

entries.cultural_note: 十三邀 is unusual in Chinese media for its willingness to sit with uncomfortable silences and unanswered questions. Xu Zhiyuan’s interview style is influenced by Western longform journalism but filtered through Chinese literary sensibility.
entries.follow_up: This is advanced listening. Focus on Cai’s speech patterns — he speaks slowly and chooses words carefully, which makes him easier to follow than Xu despite the high-level content.

entries.title: Stories from the Newsroom: The New Editorial Policy
entries.title_zh: 编辑部的故事：新编辑方针

segment.description: The editorial staff of a struggling magazine learn that leadership wants to modernize. The chief editor tries to implement changes while his staff find creative ways to resist without openly refusing. Every conversation has a surface meaning and an actual meaning.
segment.description_zh: 一本困难杂志的编辑部得知领导要改革。主编试图推行变化，员工们想出各种办法抗拥而不明着拒绝。每段对话都有表面意思和实际意思。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '编辑部的故事 第一集'
search_terms: Stories from the Newsroom Chinese sitcom

questions.q_en: How do the staff deal with the reforms?

options.text_en: Agree on the surface, resist underneath

questions.explanation: The genius of 编辑部的故事 is that passive resistance is the comedy. Nobody says no — they say yes and then do nothing, which is a deeply recognizable Chinese workplace dynamic.
改革 combines 改 (change) + 革 (leather/transform). It’s the word used for Deng Xiaoping’s 改革开放 (Reform and Opening Up), so it carries political weight beyond its dictionary meaning.

entries.cultural_note: This show is the other pillar of early Chinese sitcom alongside 我爱我家. Where that show satirized family life, this one targeted workplace bureaucracy. The double-meaning dialogue style directly influenced later shows like 武林外传.
entries.follow_up: Pay attention to the phrase “研究研究” (let’s study it / look into it). In Chinese bureaucratic language, it usually means “we will do nothing.” This show makes that subtext into comedy.

entries.title: U Can U Bibi: Should You Tell an Ugly Truth or a Beautiful Lie?
entries.title_zh: 奇葩说：说丑陋的真话还是漂亮的假话

segment.description: Two teams debate whether it’s better to tell a harsh truth or a kind lie. The debaters use personal stories, philosophical arguments, and rapid-fire wit. The audience votes shift multiple times as the lead changes. Ma Dong moderates with his signature gentle chaos.
segment.description_zh: 两队辩论说丑陋的真话和漂亮的假话哪个更好。辩手们用个人故事、哲学论证和快速机智反击。观众投票多次变化。马东用他标志性的温柔混乱主持。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '奇葩说 真话假话'
search_terms: 奇葩说 说丑陋的真话
U Can U Bibi truth vs lie debate

questions.q_zh: 这场辩论的核心问题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the core question of this debate?

options.text_en: Whether truth or lies are better

questions.explanation: The show’s format forces debaters to argue positions they may not personally hold, which produces creative reasoning and unexpected emotional honesty.
辩论 combines 辩 (argue/distinguish) + 论 (discuss/theory). It implies structured, reasoned debate rather than emotional arguing (吵架).

entries.cultural_note: 奇葩说 broke new ground by bringing debate culture to Chinese entertainment TV. The show is significant because public disagreement is culturally fraught in China — the format gives permission to argue openly, which is why it became a phenomenon.
entries.follow_up: Listen for rhetorical transitions: “换句话说” (in other words), “举个例子” (for example), “反过来说” (conversely). These are the building blocks of Chinese argumentative speech.

entries.title: iPartment: Moving Day Chaos
entries.title_zh: 爱情公寓：搞笑的搬家日

segment.description: New tenants move into a Shanghai apartment building. The episode introduces each roommate through a series of slapstick encounters in the hallway and shared spaces. The humor is broad and physical, with fast dialogue and pop culture references.
segment.description_zh: 新租客搬进一栋上海公寓楼。每个室友通过走廊和公共空间的一系列搞笑相遇登场。幽默风格夸张、对话快、充满流行文化梨。

where_to_find.primary: YouTube: search '爱情公寓 第一集'

questions.q_en: In which city does the story take place?
questions.explanation: The show is set in Shanghai, and the characters represent different types of young urban professionals. Shanghai’s modernity and international flavor are part of the show’s identity.
室友 combines 室 (room) + 友 (friend). It specifically means someone you share a room or apartment with, common in Chinese university and urban life.

entries.cultural_note: 爱情公寓 is China’s most popular ensemble sitcom among younger viewers. It borrows heavily from American sitcom structure (Friends, How I Met Your Mother) but localizes the humor to Chinese urban youth culture — dating apps, high rent, parental pressure.
entries.follow_up: This is good HSK 3-4 listening because the dialogue is fast but uses everyday vocabulary. Try watching without subtitles first, then with Chinese subtitles to fill gaps.
